AI Agents

25 posts with the tag “AI Agents”

Harness Engineering: OpenAI's 8 Rules for an Agent-First World

Mar 5, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Harness Engineering: OpenAI’s 8 Rules for an Agent-First World

Section titled “Harness Engineering: OpenAI’s 8 Rules for an Agent-First World”

The 800-Line File That Had to Die

Section titled “The 800-Line File That Had to Die”

A team at OpenAI killed their 800-line AGENTS.md file. Not because it was wrong — because it was too right. The agents couldn’t find what they needed in a wall of context.

They replaced it with a 100-line table of contents pointing to structured files in a docs/ directory. That single decision captures what’s happening to software engineering right now.

The engineers getting the most out of AI agents aren’t writing better prompts or picking smarter models. They’re redesigning the engineering environment itself — repo structure, linter rules, merge gates, documentation layout — so agents can operate independently.

OpenAI calls this “harness engineering.” Muratcan Koylan distilled the key patterns from OpenAI’s published practices, and what emerges looks nothing like how most teams work today.

The 8 Principles

Section titled “The 8 Principles”

1. Engineers Become Environment Designers

Section titled “1. Engineers Become Environment Designers”

The job shifted. It’s no longer about writing code — it’s about designing systems, specifying intent, and building feedback loops.

When something fails, the question isn’t “try harder.” It’s “what capability is missing?”

Human time and attention are the only scarce resources left. Everything else can be delegated — if the environment is right.

2. Give Agents a Map, Not an Encyclopedia

Section titled “2. Give Agents a Map, Not an Encyclopedia”

Progressive disclosure beats comprehensive documentation. Every time.

A ~100-line AGENTS.md as a table of contents, pointing to a structured docs/ directory, outperforms a massive all-in-one reference. Too much context crowds out the actual task.

The same pattern that works for onboarding human engineers works for agents. Maybe even better, because agents follow what they can see and ignore what they can’t.

3. If Agents Can’t See It, It Doesn’t Exist

Section titled “3. If Agents Can’t See It, It Doesn’t Exist”

Slack threads. Google Docs. Hallway conversations. Tribal knowledge passed down through pair programming sessions.

All invisible to agents.

If knowledge isn’t encoded as versioned, discoverable artifacts in the repo, it effectively doesn’t exist for your fastest workers. Think of it as onboarding practices — but for non-human engineers who will never ask a clarifying question over coffee.

4. Enforce Architecture Mechanically, Not Through Instructions

Section titled “4. Enforce Architecture Mechanically, Not Through Instructions”

This one is sharp.

Custom linters with remediation instructions baked into error messages. The error output itself becomes agent context. An agent hits a boundary violation, reads the error message, and knows exactly how to fix it — without consulting any documentation.

Strict layered architecture with validated dependency directions. The principle: enforce boundaries centrally, allow autonomy locally.

Telling agents “don’t do X” in a markdown file is a suggestion. Making X trigger a build failure is a rule.

5. Boring Technology Wins

Section titled “5. Boring Technology Wins”

Composable, stable APIs with strong training-set representation outperform cutting-edge libraries that agents struggle with.

Agents have been trained on mountains of React, Express, and PostgreSQL code. They’ve seen far less of your team’s custom framework or that niche library with 200 GitHub stars.

Sometimes it’s cheaper to reimplement a subset of functionality than fight opaque upstream behavior. Pick what agents already know.

6. Entropy Management Is Garbage Collection

Section titled “6. Entropy Management Is Garbage Collection”

Agents replicate existing patterns. All of them. Including the bad ones.

That one hacky workaround from six months ago? An agent will find it, treat it as the canonical pattern, and reproduce it across 40 files before anyone notices.

Without active maintenance, code quality degrades fast. The solution: recurring background agents that scan for deviations from established patterns and auto-fix. Continuous garbage collection for your codebase.

7. Throughput Changes Merge Philosophy

Section titled “7. Throughput Changes Merge Philosophy”

Minimal blocking merge gates. Short-lived PRs. Test flakes addressed with follow-up runs, not by blocking merges.

The principle: corrections are cheap, waiting is expensive.

When agents can generate and test changes faster than humans can review them, the bottleneck moves from code production to code approval. Redesign the pipeline for throughput, not for gatekeeping.

8. Agent-to-Agent Code Review

Section titled “8. Agent-to-Agent Code Review”

This is where it gets interesting.

OpenAI pushed almost all code review to agent-to-agent loops. Codex reviews its own changes, requests additional agent reviews, iterates until satisfied. Humans are escalated to only when judgment is required.

Read that again. The code review loop — something most teams consider a core human activity — has been restructured. Not eliminated. The human role shifted from reviewing every change to designing review criteria and handling edge cases.

The Pattern Underneath

Section titled “The Pattern Underneath”

Across all eight principles, the same thing happened. Nothing disappeared. Everything shifted.

The engineer didn’t become irrelevant. The engineer’s job description changed. From code author to environment designer. From reviewer to review-system designer. From architect to architecture-enforcement designer.

The code still gets written. The architecture still gets designed. The reviews still happen. But the human’s primary output shifted from code artifacts to environment artifacts — documentation structure, linter rules, merge policies, feedback loops.

Where to Start

Section titled “Where to Start”

The question isn’t whether this shift will reach your team. It’s whether you’ll design for it or react to it.

Start with one audit: can a new agent, with zero context beyond what’s in the repo, find what it needs, follow the architectural rules, and ship working code?

If not, you have your backlog.

A few concrete first moves:

  • Audit your AGENTS.md (or equivalent). Is it a wall of text or a navigable map? Trim it to a table of contents pointing to structured docs.
  • Check for invisible knowledge. What do new hires learn in their first week that isn’t written down? That’s your agent knowledge gap too.
  • Add remediation hints to your linter rules. Every error message is a teaching opportunity for agents.
  • Pick one pattern violation that keeps recurring and write a background cleanup script. Automate the janitor.
  • Try agent-to-agent review on a low-risk PR. Set up a second agent session to review the first agent’s output before a human looks at it.

References

Section titled “References”
  • OpenAI: Harness Engineering — the original post on leveraging Codex in an agent-first world
  • Muratcan Koylan’s thread — the distilled 8-point summary that inspired this analysis
Tags:
  • AI Agents
  • Agent Orchestration
  • OpenAI
  • Codex
  • Harness Engineering
  • Software Engineering
  • Developer Workflow

Paperclip: Your AI Agents Need a Company, Not Better Prompts

Mar 5, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Paperclip: Your AI Agents Need a Company, Not Better Prompts

Section titled “Paperclip: Your AI Agents Need a Company, Not Better Prompts”

The Missing Layer

Section titled “The Missing Layer”

You can download a company now. Not a template. Not a tutorial. A functioning business with a CEO, a CTO, engineers, and a marketing team — all AI agents, organized under an org chart, governed by budgets, pointed at a single mission.

One command. That’s it. Paperclip is MIT-licensed, self-hosted, and needs no account. The interesting question isn’t whether AI can do the work — it’s who manages the office.

The Real Problem Nobody Talks About

Section titled “The Real Problem Nobody Talks About”

Everyone focused on building smarter agents. Longer context windows. Better tool use. More autonomous sessions. And the agents did get better. Claude writes production code. Codex ships features. OpenClaw runs for hours without supervision.

But here’s what actually happens when you scale up:

  • You have 20 Claude Code tabs open. You can’t remember which one is doing what.
  • You reboot your machine. Everything vanishes.
  • A runaway loop burns through $200 in tokens before you notice.
  • You spend 15 minutes gathering context just to remind an agent what the project is about.
  • You’re basically reinventing project management with sticky notes and folder names.

The agents aren’t the bottleneck. The lack of organizational structure is.

Think About How Human Companies Work

Section titled “Think About How Human Companies Work”

When you hire 20 people, you don’t throw them in a room and say “build something.” You give them:

  • An org chart with reporting lines
  • Job descriptions with clear responsibilities
  • Budgets with spending limits
  • Goals that cascade from a company mission down to individual tasks
  • A system for accountability and oversight

AI agents need the same things. Paperclip provides them.

What Paperclip Actually Does

Section titled “What Paperclip Actually Does”

Paperclip is a Node.js server with a React dashboard. It orchestrates AI agents into a functioning organization. The setup follows three steps:

StepWhat You DoExample
01Define the goal”Build the #1 AI note-taking app to $1M MRR”
02Hire the teamCEO (Claude), CTO (Cursor), Engineers (Codex), Marketing (OpenClaw)
03Approve and runReview strategy, set budgets, hit go

From there, agents operate on their own. They wake on scheduled heartbeats, check their task queue, delegate up and down the org chart, and work. Every decision gets logged. Every tool call gets traced. Sessions persist across reboots.

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”

Bring Your Own Agent

Section titled “Bring Your Own Agent”

Paperclip is unopinionated about agent runtimes. If it can receive a heartbeat signal, it’s hired. Claude Code sessions, OpenClaw bots, Python scripts, shell commands, HTTP webhooks — anything works.

Agent TypeWorks?
OpenClawYes
Claude CodeYes
CodexYes
CursorYes
Bash scriptsYes
HTTP webhooksYes

Cost Control That Actually Works

Section titled “Cost Control That Actually Works”

Every agent gets a monthly budget. At 80% utilization, you get a warning. At 100%, the agent auto-pauses and new tasks are blocked. You can override the limit at any time.

Track costs per agent, per task, per project, per goal. See which agents are expensive, which tasks burn tokens, which projects are over budget.

Multi-Company Support

Section titled “Multi-Company Support”

One Paperclip deployment can run dozens of isolated businesses. A crypto trading desk with 12 agents. A TikTok content factory with 5. An e-commerce operation with 10. Complete data isolation between them. One control plane for everything.

Governance

Section titled “Governance”

You operate as the board of directors. Agents can’t hire new agents without your approval. The CEO can’t execute a strategy you haven’t reviewed. You can pause, override, reassign, or terminate any agent at any time.

Autonomy is a privilege you grant, not a default.

Ticket System with Full Tracing

Section titled “Ticket System with Full Tracing”

Every instruction, every response, every tool call and decision is recorded. Append-only audit log — no edits, no deletions.

Goal Alignment

Section titled “Goal Alignment”

Every task traces back to the company mission. Agents don’t just know what to do — they know why. Context flows from the mission through projects down to individual tasks automatically via Paperclip’s SKILLS.md.

What Paperclip Is Not

Section titled “What Paperclip Is Not”

This is worth being clear about:

  • Not a chatbot. Agents have jobs, not chat windows.
  • Not an agent framework. It doesn’t tell you how to build agents. It tells you how to run a company made of them.
  • Not a workflow builder. No drag-and-drop pipelines. It models companies.
  • Not a prompt manager. Agents bring their own prompts, models, and runtimes.
  • Not a single-agent tool. If you have one agent, you probably don’t need this. If you have twenty, you do.

Technical Details

Section titled “Technical Details”

Paperclip runs as a single Node.js process locally, with an embedded PostgreSQL database created automatically. No external dependencies for getting started.

The API server runs at http://localhost:3100. Requirements: Node.js 20+, pnpm 9.15+.

For production, point it at your own Postgres and deploy however you like. Solo entrepreneurs can use Tailscale for remote access, then move to Vercel or similar when needed.

Under the Hood

Section titled “Under the Hood”
  • Atomic execution — Task checkout and budget enforcement are atomic. No double-work, no runaway spend.
  • Persistent agent state — Agents resume the same task context across heartbeats instead of restarting from scratch.
  • Runtime skill injection — Agents learn Paperclip workflows at runtime without retraining.
  • Governance with rollback — Config changes are versioned. Bad changes can be rolled back.
  • Portable company templates — Export/import orgs, agents, and skills with secret scrubbing.

Coming Soon: Clipmart

Section titled “Coming Soon: Clipmart”

The team is building a marketplace for company templates. Browse pre-built org structures — a crypto trading desk, a YouTube content factory, a dev agency — and import them into your Paperclip instance in seconds.

Download a business. Import. Run.

Who Should Use This

Section titled “Who Should Use This”
  • You coordinate many different agents (OpenClaw, Codex, Claude, Cursor) toward a common goal
  • You have 20 simultaneous Claude Code terminals and lose track of what everyone is doing
  • You want agents running autonomously 24/7 but still want to audit work when needed
  • You want to monitor costs and enforce budgets
  • You want to manage autonomous businesses from your phone

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

The interactive setup walks you through database configuration and creating your first company. From there, define your goal, hire your agents, and let Paperclip handle the coordination.

  • GitHub: paperclipai/paperclip
  • Docs: paperclip.ing/docs
  • Discord: discord.gg/m4HZY7xNG3
  • License: MIT

The agents are ready. Give them a company to work in.

Tags:
  • AI Agents
  • Agent Orchestration
  • Open Source
  • Paperclip
  • Multi-Agent
  • Autonomous Business
  • Node.js

BMAD v6.0.4: Two Minutes from Beta to Stable

Mar 2, 2026
Vibe Sparking

One Command, Two Minutes

Section titled “One Command, Two Minutes”

You type bunx bmad-method install. The installer sees your beta 8 setup and offers a Quick Update. Two minutes later, you have a stable platform with 82 workflows, 27 agents, and configurations for 28 AI tools. Your existing settings are untouched.

That’s the whole upgrade story. But the details are worth knowing.

What Changed: Beta 8 to v6.0.4

Section titled “What Changed: Beta 8 to v6.0.4”

BMAD v6.0.4 is the first official stable release. The “beta” label is gone.

The core and all five modules (bmm, bmb, cis, gds, tea) got updates. The TEA module is the big addition - it brings automated testing into the BMAD ecosystem. The tool count jumped from 18 to 28, covering nearly every AI coding assistant on the market.

Here’s what’s new:

WhatBefore (Beta 8)After (v6.0.4)
Release statusBetaStable
Modules56 (TEA added)
Workflows~7482
AI tool integrations1828
Contract testing-Pact.js + SmartBear MCP
Browser testing-Playwright CLI + MCP
Stack detectionManualAuto-detect
CI platform detectionManualAuto-detect

The Upgrade Process

Section titled “The Upgrade Process”

If you already have BMAD installed, run:

The installer detects your existing installation:

Pick Quick Update. That’s the key decision.

The installer checks each module. In my case, all five existing modules were already structurally up to date - the update applied configuration refinements and the new TEA module setup.

TEA Module: Testing Built In

Section titled “TEA Module: Testing Built In”

The TEA (Test Architect) module is the standout addition. During upgrade, it asks a few questions:

Pact.js contract testing - If you work with microservices, enable this. It integrates @seontechnologies/pactjs-utils for consumer-driven contract testing, with both local monorepo and remote broker workflows.

SmartBear MCP - Connects to PactFlow for broker queries. Requires PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL and PACT_BROKER_TOKEN as env vars.

Execution mode - TEA can orchestrate multi-step generation using agent-teams, subagents, or sequential fallback. The “Auto” option probes your runtime and picks the best mode.

Stack auto-detection - TEA reads your project files to determine if you’re running frontend, backend, or full-stack:

  • Frontend: package.json with React/Vue/Angular, playwright.config.*
  • Backend: pyproject.toml, pom.xml, go.mod, *.csproj
  • Full-stack: Both indicators present

CI platform auto-detection - Checks for .github/workflows/, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile, and other CI config files.

Playwright Integration

Section titled “Playwright Integration”

TEA includes Playwright setup for browser testing:

Two MCP servers can be configured:

  1. playwright - npx @playwright/mcp@latest
  2. playwright-test - npx playwright run-test-mcp-server

After the Upgrade

Section titled “After the Upgrade”

The final output tells the full story:

28 AI tools now have BMAD configurations. That includes the original 18 plus newer additions: Kilo, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder, Rovo Dev, Trae, Antigravity, iFlow, and others.

The last line is the one that matters: preserved settings. Your name, language, output directory, module preferences - all carried over.

The Full Tool Support List

Section titled “The Full Tool Support List”

BMAD v6.0.4 generates configurations for:

  • Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Windsurf (top tier)
  • Auggie, Cline, Crush, Gemini CLI
  • GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity, iFlow
  • Kilo Code, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder
  • Roo Cline, Rovo Dev, Trae

Each tool gets its own configuration directory with 27 agents, 74 workflows, and 7 tasks.

First Time? Start Here

Section titled “First Time? Start Here”

If you’re installing BMAD fresh:

Follow the interactive prompts. Pick at least the BMM and BMB modules. Add CIS if you do creative work, GDS for game dev, TEA for testing.

After installation, run /bmad-help in your AI coding tool to see what’s available.

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • Docs: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/
  • Changelog: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
  • YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@BMadCode
  • Discord: https://discord.gg/gk8jAdXWmj

BMAD is 100% free. No paywalls, no gated Discord. If you find it useful, star the repo or subscribe to the YouTube channel.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Development
  • Vibe Coding
  • AI Agents
  • Testing

BMAD v6.0.0-beta-8 Installation Guide: Building Your AI Development Team from Scratch

Feb 14, 2026
Vibe Sparking

From Alpha to Beta: BMAD Has Grown Up

Section titled “From Alpha to Beta: BMAD Has Grown Up”

BMAD v6.0.0-beta-8 has been released. This isn’t just another update—BMAD has officially graduated from Alpha.

What does this mean?

  • 50+ workflows covering the complete development lifecycle
  • Stable architecture with no anticipated breaking changes
  • Support for custom modules and community extensions

More importantly: 100% free, no paywalls, no gated Discord.

Two Installation Methods: Which to Choose?

Section titled “Two Installation Methods: Which to Choose?”

Non-Interactive: For CI/CD Pipelines

Section titled “Non-Interactive: For CI/CD Pipelines”

If you’re installing in an automated environment, use command-line flags:

This command automatically installs:

  • BMM Module: Business Method Module for IT projects
  • BMB Module: BMad Builder for extending the platform

Then generates configuration files for 18 AI coding tools.

Interactive: For First-Time Users

Section titled “Interactive: For First-Time Users”

If you want to understand each option, run:

Then follow the prompts step by step.

Interactive Installation Walkthrough

Section titled “Interactive Installation Walkthrough”

Step 1: Choose Installation Directory

Section titled “Step 1: Choose Installation Directory”

The installer detects the current directory:

Confirm to continue.

Step 2: Select Modules

Section titled “Step 2: Select Modules”

BMAD offers 5 optional modules:

ModuleCodePurpose
BMad Method Agile-AI Driven-DevelopmentbmmTraditional software development
BMad BuilderbmbCreate custom agents and workflows
BMad Creative Intelligence SuitecisCreative writing, brainstorming
BMad Game Dev StudiogdsGame development
Test ArchitectteaTest strategy and quality assurance

My recommendation: Install at least bmm and bmb for your first time. Add cis if you write blogs or do creative work.

Step 3: Select AI Tool Integrations

Section titled “Step 3: Select AI Tool Integrations”

BMAD supports 18 AI coding tools:

  • ⭐ Claude Code (recommended)
  • ⭐ Codex
  • ⭐ Cursor
  • ⭐ Windsurf
  • Auggie
  • Cline
  • Crush
  • Gemini CLI
  • GitHub Copilot
  • Google Antigravity
  • iFlow
  • Kilo Code
  • Kiro
  • OpenCode
  • QwenCoder
  • Roo Cline
  • Rovo Dev
  • Trae

Choose the tools you actually use. Each tool gets its own configuration folder (like .claude/, .cursor/).

Step 4: Core Configuration

Section titled “Step 4: Core Configuration”

These settings affect how agents interact with you and where generated documents are saved.

Step 5: Module Configuration

Section titled “Step 5: Module Configuration”

Each module asks if you want to accept defaults:

Choose Yes for first-time installation. You can manually edit config files later if needed.

Installation Complete: What You Get

Section titled “Installation Complete: What You Get”

After installation, you’ll see a summary like this:

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Q: Installation fails with IDE 'kiro-cli' handler not found

Section titled “Q: Installation fails with IDE 'kiro-cli' handler not found”

Remove the kiro-cli module from the installation command.

Q: Installation fails with Source for module 'bmgd' is not available

Section titled “Q: Installation fails with Source for module 'bmgd' is not available”

Remove bmgd and cis modules, then re-run the installation command.

After successful installation, you can add other modules:

After Installation: Next Steps

Section titled “After Installation: Next Steps”
  1. Run /bmad-help to see available commands and workflows
  2. Choose an agent to start your first task
  3. Read the docs at http://docs.bmad-method.org/

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • Documentation: http://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/
  • Changelog: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/CHANGELOG.md
  • YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@BMadCode

The BMAD team believes knowledge should be shared, not sold. If you find it useful, give them a Star or subscribe to their YouTube channel.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Development
  • Vibe Coding
  • AI Agents

OpenClaw Telegram Topics: Run Parallel Task Flows in One Group

Feb 1, 2026
Vibe Sparking

OpenClaw Telegram Topics: Run Parallel Task Flows in One Group

Section titled “OpenClaw Telegram Topics: Run Parallel Task Flows in One Group”

OpenClaw is an open-source AI assistant that connects to Telegram, Discord, and other platforms. Its “concurrent task engine” capability shines when paired with Telegram’s Topics feature. This guide walks through setting up multiple isolated task flows in a single group.

Understanding OpenClaw’s Concurrency Model

Section titled “Understanding OpenClaw’s Concurrency Model”

OpenClaw processes tasks through Sessions:

  • One Session = one independent task flow with its own context
  • Multiple Sessions = OpenClaw handling concurrent tasks without cross-contamination

In Telegram, each Topic becomes a Session. One group can contain Chat, Work, and Feed Topics—each maintaining separate conversations and contexts.

Session Key Architecture

Section titled “Session Key Architecture”

OpenClaw generates session keys based on chat location:

LocationSession Key Pattern
DMsShared main session
Groupsagent:<agentId>:telegram:group:<chatId>
Topicsagent:<agentId>:telegram:group:<chatId>:topic:<threadId>

The :topic:<threadId> suffix isolates each Topic’s context. Messages in Topic A never bleed into Topic B.

Setting Up Parallel Task Lanes

Section titled “Setting Up Parallel Task Lanes”

Recommended Topic Structure

Section titled “Recommended Topic Structure”

Start with three Topics for common workflows:

TopicPurposeUse Case
ChatDaily conversationCasual questions, quick answers
WorkBusiness tasksDocument processing, data analysis
FeedInformation patrolNews aggregation, monitoring

Step 1: Enable Bot Group Visibility

Section titled “Step 1: Enable Bot Group Visibility”

Telegram’s Privacy Mode blocks bots from seeing group messages by default. Fix this:

  1. Open @BotFather
  2. Send /setprivacy
  3. Select your bot
  4. Choose Disable
  5. Remove and re-add the bot to your group
  6. Grant admin permissions

Admin status guarantees message visibility regardless of privacy settings.

Step 2: Enable Topics in Your Group

Section titled “Step 2: Enable Topics in Your Group”
  1. Open group settings
  2. Find Topics or Forum mode
  3. Toggle it on
  4. Create your Chat, Work, and Feed Topics

Step 3: Configure Per-Topic Behavior

Section titled “Step 3: Configure Per-Topic Behavior”

Edit your openclaw.json configuration:

Replace -1001234567890 with your group ID (forward any message to @userinfobot to get it). Replace 123 and 456 with your Topic thread IDs.

Configuration Options Explained

Section titled “Configuration Options Explained”
OptionValuesEffect
requireMentiontrue/falseWhether bot needs @mention to respond
systemPromptstringAdditional instructions for this Topic
skillsarrayLimit available skills per Topic
allowFromarrayRestrict senders in this Topic

Topics inherit parent group settings unless explicitly overridden.

Testing Your Setup

Section titled “Testing Your Setup”

Quick validation checklist:

  1. Send in Chat Topic: Bot should respond only when @mentioned (if requireMention: true)
  2. Send in Work Topic: Bot should respond to all messages (if requireMention: false)
  3. Check isolation: Reset one Topic’s session with /new—other Topics should retain their context

Common Issues and Fixes

Section titled “Common Issues and Fixes”

Bot Not Responding in Group

Section titled “Bot Not Responding in Group”

Cause: Privacy Mode still enabled or bot lacks admin status.

Fix: Re-run /setprivacy → Disable in @BotFather. Ensure bot is group admin.

DMs Work But Group Doesn’t

Section titled “DMs Work But Group Doesn’t”

Cause: Combination of privacy settings and requireMention misconfiguration.

Fix:

  1. Verify admin status
  2. Check requireMention value matches your intent
  3. Try explicit @mention first to confirm bot sees messages

Responses Appearing in Wrong Topic

Section titled “Responses Appearing in Wrong Topic”

Cause: Topic ID misconfigured in JSON.

Fix: Use @userinfobot to verify exact Topic thread IDs. Update config with correct values.

Session Reset Affecting Multiple Topics

Section titled “Session Reset Affecting Multiple Topics”

Known Issue: In multi-agent setups, /new on a non-default agent may reset both its session and the default agent’s session.

Workaround: Use single-agent setup per group or track this GitHub issue for patches.

Advanced: Multi-Agent with Separate Bots

Section titled “Advanced: Multi-Agent with Separate Bots”

For complex workflows, run multiple OpenClaw instances:

Each account maintains completely separate sessions and configurations.

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”
  1. Start simple: Use three Topics maximum until workflow patterns stabilize
  2. Topic naming: Clear names help both humans and the bot understand context
  3. Selective mentions: Keep requireMention: true for high-traffic Topics to avoid spam
  4. History limits: Adjust historyLimit per Topic for context-heavy workflows

References

Section titled “References”
  • OpenClaw Official Site
  • OpenClaw Telegram Documentation
  • OpenClaw GitHub Repository
  • Telegram Bot Privacy Settings
  • Original Twitter Thread by @Tz_2022
Tags:
  • AI
  • OpenClaw
  • Telegram
  • AI Agents
  • Automation

Principles of Building AI Agents: The Most Popular AI Book in San Francisco

Jan 31, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Principles of Building AI Agents

Section titled “Principles of Building AI Agents”

A Y Combinator founder called it “the most popular book in San Francisco.” With over 119,500 copies distributed globally and lines at AI meetups for physical copies, Principles of Building AI Agents by Sam Bhagwat has become the defining resource for engineers building production-ready agentic systems.

Why This Book Matters

Section titled “Why This Book Matters”

The AI agent landscape moves fast. New frameworks emerge weekly. APIs change monthly. Most resources become outdated before they’re published.

This book takes a different approach. Instead of framework-specific tutorials, it teaches fundamental patterns that apply regardless of your stack. Whether you’re using LangChain, CrewAI, Mastra, or building from scratch, the architectural decisions remain the same.

About the Author

Section titled “About the Author”

Sam Bhagwat is the founder of Gatsby (the React framework that pioneered modern static site generation) and now CEO of Mastra.ai, the TypeScript framework for building AI agents.

His background building developer tools gives the book a practical edge—these aren’t theoretical patterns but battle-tested approaches from real production systems.

Book Structure: 34 Chapters Across 6 Domains

Section titled “Book Structure: 34 Chapters Across 6 Domains”

The second edition spans 34 chapters organized into six major sections:

1. Foundations (Chapters 1-5)

Section titled “1. Foundations (Chapters 1-5)”
  • LLM history and evolution
  • Provider and model selection criteria
  • Prompt engineering fundamentals
  • Token economics and cost optimization
  • API patterns and best practices

2. Agent Mechanics (Chapters 6-12)

Section titled “2. Agent Mechanics (Chapters 6-12)”
  • Agents 101: Core concepts and terminology
  • Model routing for multi-model architectures
  • Tool calling patterns and function definitions
  • Memory systems: short-term, long-term, episodic
  • Agent state management

3. Advanced Patterns (Chapters 13-19)

Section titled “3. Advanced Patterns (Chapters 13-19)”
  • Dynamic agents that adapt at runtime
  • Middleware patterns for cross-cutting concerns
  • Guardrails and safety mechanisms
  • Authentication and authorization
  • Error handling and recovery strategies

4. Workflows (Chapters 20-26)

Section titled “4. Workflows (Chapters 20-26)”
  • Branching and conditional logic
  • Chaining operations
  • Merging results from parallel execution
  • Conditions and decision trees
  • Streaming responses
  • Observability and debugging

5. RAG Systems (Chapters 27-31)

Section titled “5. RAG Systems (Chapters 27-31)”
  • Vector databases comparison
  • Chunking strategies
  • Embedding models and selection
  • Query optimization
  • When to use alternatives to RAG

6. Multi-Agent & Deployment (Chapters 32-34)

Section titled “6. Multi-Agent & Deployment (Chapters 32-34)”
  • Supervisor patterns for orchestrating agents
  • Control flow between agents
  • Standards comparison: A2A vs. MCP
  • Evals and testing
  • Local development to production deployment
  • Multimodal capabilities
  • Code generation patterns

What Makes This Book Different

Section titled “What Makes This Book Different”

Practical Over Theoretical

Section titled “Practical Over Theoretical”

Every chapter includes code examples. Not pseudocode or simplified demonstrations—actual implementations you can run. The examples use Mastra but concepts translate to any framework.

Framework-Agnostic Principles

Section titled “Framework-Agnostic Principles”

The book focuses on why decisions matter, not just how to implement them. Understanding trade-offs between vector search vs. keyword search matters more than memorizing a specific API.

Rapid Updates

Section titled “Rapid Updates”

Life moves fast in AI. The second edition shipped just two months after the first, adding coverage for:

  • MCP (Model Context Protocol)
  • Image generation
  • Voice interfaces
  • Agent-to-Agent communication (A2A)
  • Web browsing and computer use
  • Workflow streaming
  • Agentic RAG patterns

Free and Accessible

Section titled “Free and Accessible”

The complete book is available free at mastra.ai/book. Physical copies are available on Amazon for those who prefer paper.

Key Concepts Explored

Section titled “Key Concepts Explored”

Modular Architecture

Section titled “Modular Architecture”

The book emphasizes building agents from composable components:

  • Providers: The LLM connection layer
  • Models: Specific model instances with configurations
  • Prompts: Templated instructions with variables
  • Tools: External capabilities agents can invoke
  • Memory: Persistence and context management

This modularity allows swapping components without rewriting entire systems.

Memory as First-Class Citizen

Section titled “Memory as First-Class Citizen”

Unlike frameworks that treat memory as an afterthought, the book dedicates multiple chapters to memory architecture:

  • Working memory: Current conversation context
  • Episodic memory: Past interactions and outcomes
  • Semantic memory: Learned facts and relationships
  • Procedural memory: How to accomplish tasks

Guardrails Without Paralysis

Section titled “Guardrails Without Paralysis”

Safety matters, but over-constrained agents become useless. The book provides frameworks for:

  • Input validation without blocking legitimate requests
  • Output filtering that preserves utility
  • Rate limiting and abuse prevention
  • Graceful degradation when components fail

Who Should Read This

Section titled “Who Should Read This”

Backend Engineers building AI features into existing products. The book bridges the gap between ML concepts and production engineering.

Startup Founders evaluating AI architecture decisions. Understanding trade-offs early prevents expensive rewrites later.

Product Managers working with AI teams. The conceptual chapters provide vocabulary for productive technical discussions.

ML Engineers transitioning to agent development. Many ML patterns don’t apply—this book explains what changes and why.

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”
  1. Download the book: mastra.ai/book (free, email required)
  2. Join the community: The Discord has active discussions on implementation challenges
  3. Try the examples: Clone the companion repository and run the code

The Bigger Picture

Section titled “The Bigger Picture”

AI agents are moving from demos to production. The companies that will win aren’t those with the most sophisticated models—they’re the ones who can ship reliable, maintainable agent systems.

This book provides the foundation. Not by prescribing specific tools, but by teaching the thinking that makes good agent architectures possible.

“Principles of Building AI Agents” isn’t the only resource you’ll need. But it’s the one that helps everything else make sense.

References

Section titled “References”
  • Principles of Building AI Agents - Official Book
  • Announcing the Second Edition
  • Mastra.ai - TypeScript AI Agent Framework
  • Amazon Physical Copy
  • Sam Bhagwat on X (@calcsam)
  • Sumanth on X (@Sumanth_077)
Tags:
  • AI
  • AI Agents
  • LLM
  • Open Source
  • Mastra
  • TypeScript

Vercel Agent Skills: Official Skills Collection for AI Coding Agents

Jan 28, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

As AI coding assistants become increasingly capable, developers are finding new ways to extend these agents with domain-specific knowledge. Vercel Labs has released an official collection of agent skills that encapsulates years of engineering expertise into machine-readable formats that AI agents can understand and apply.

The vercel-labs/agent-skills repository provides production-ready skills covering React optimization, web design guidelines, React Native patterns, and React composition patterns.

What Are Agent Skills?

Section titled “What Are Agent Skills?”

Agent Skills are packaged instructions and scripts that extend AI coding agent capabilities. Unlike traditional documentation, skills are specifically formatted for LLM consumption, enabling agents to:

  • Parse and understand rules automatically
  • Apply patterns when writing or reviewing code
  • Detect relevant contexts and activate accordingly
  • Execute automation scripts when needed

Vercel’s skills follow the Agent Skills format, ensuring compatibility across multiple AI coding platforms.

Available Skills

Section titled “Available Skills”

1. React Best Practices

Section titled “1. React Best Practices”

react-best-practices contains 40+ rules across 8 categories, distilled from Vercel Engineering’s decade of React and Next.js optimization experience.

Use when:

  • Writing new React components or Next.js pages
  • Implementing data fetching (client or server-side)
  • Reviewing code for performance issues
  • Optimizing bundle size or load times

Categories covered:

  • Eliminating waterfalls (Critical) - Parallel data fetching, prefetching
  • Bundle size optimization (Critical) - Tree shaking, code splitting, dynamic imports
  • Server-side performance (High) - Server components, streaming, caching
  • Client-side data fetching (Medium-High) - SWR/React Query patterns
  • Re-render optimization (Medium) - Memoization, useMemo, useCallback
  • Rendering performance (Medium) - Virtualization, CSS containment
  • JavaScript micro-optimizations (Low-Medium) - Event handlers, property access

2. Web Design Guidelines

Section titled “2. Web Design Guidelines”

web-design-guidelines audits UI code for 100+ rules covering accessibility, performance, and UX best practices.

Use when:

  • “Review my UI”
  • “Check accessibility”
  • “Audit design”
  • “Review UX”
  • “Check my site against best practices”

Categories covered:

  • Accessibility - ARIA labels, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation
  • Focus States - Visible focus indicators, focus-visible patterns
  • Forms - Autocomplete, validation, error handling
  • Animation - Prefers-reduced-motion, compositor-friendly transforms
  • Typography - Curly quotes, ellipsis, tabular nums
  • Images - Dimensions, lazy loading, alt text
  • Performance - Virtualization, layout thrashing prevention, preconnect
  • Navigation & State - URL reflects state, deep-linking
  • Dark Mode & Theming - Color-scheme, theme-color meta
  • Touch & Interaction - Touch-action, tap-highlight
  • Locale & i18n - Intl.DateTimeFormat, Intl.NumberFormat

3. React Native Guidelines

Section titled “3. React Native Guidelines”

react-native-guidelines contains 16 rules across 7 sections covering mobile performance, architecture, and platform-specific patterns.

Use when:

  • Building React Native or Expo apps
  • Optimizing mobile performance
  • Implementing animations or gestures
  • Working with native modules or platform APIs

Categories covered:

  • Performance (Critical) - FlashList, memoization, heavy computation offloading
  • Layout (High) - Flex patterns, safe areas, keyboard handling
  • Animation (High) - Reanimated library, gesture handling
  • Images (Medium) - expo-image, caching, lazy loading
  • State Management (Medium) - Zustand patterns, React Compiler
  • Architecture (Medium) - Monorepo structure, import optimization
  • Platform (Medium) - iOS/Android specific patterns

4. Composition Patterns

Section titled “4. Composition Patterns”

composition-patterns helps avoid boolean prop proliferation through compound components, state lifting, and internal composition.

Use when:

  • Refactoring components with many boolean props
  • Building reusable component libraries
  • Designing flexible APIs
  • Reviewing component architecture

Patterns covered:

  • Extracting compound components
  • Lifting state to reduce prop drilling
  • Composing internals for flexibility
  • Building composable APIs

5. Vercel Deploy Claimable

Section titled “5. Vercel Deploy Claimable”

vercel-deploy-claimable enables instant deployment to Vercel directly from AI conversations. Deployments are “claimable” - users can transfer ownership to their own Vercel account.

Use when:

  • “Deploy my app”
  • “Deploy this to production”
  • “Push this live”
  • “Deploy and give me the link”

Features:

  • Auto-detects 40+ frameworks from package.json
  • Returns preview URL (live site) and claim URL (transfer ownership)
  • Handles static HTML projects automatically
  • Excludes node_modules and .git from uploads

Installation

Section titled “Installation”

Install all Vercel Agent Skills with a single command:

The installer will automatically detect your installed AI agents and install skills using symlinks for easy updates. It supports 30+ AI coding agents.

Supported Agents

Section titled “Supported Agents”

Vercel Agent Skills work with:

  • Claude Code (Primary target)
  • Cursor
  • VS Code
  • GitHub Copilot
  • Windsurf
  • OpenCode
  • Codex
  • Zed
  • And 20+ more agents

Usage Examples

Section titled “Usage Examples”

Once installed, skills activate automatically when relevant contexts are detected:

React Performance Review

Section titled “React Performance Review”

The agent will load react-best-practices and analyze:

  • Waterfall elimination opportunities
  • Bundle optimization potential
  • Re-render optimization needs
  • Server component migration opportunities

UI Accessibility Audit

Section titled “UI Accessibility Audit”

The agent will load web-design-guidelines and audit:

  • ARIA label completeness
  • Keyboard navigation support
  • Focus state visibility
  • Semantic HTML usage

Mobile App Optimization

Section titled “Mobile App Optimization”

The agent will load react-native-guidelines and suggest:

  • FlashList implementation
  • Memoization strategies
  • Image optimization
  • Platform-specific patterns

Instant Deployment

Section titled “Instant Deployment”

The agent will:

  1. Package your project
  2. Detect framework from package.json
  3. Upload to Vercel
  4. Return preview and claim URLs

What Makes Vercel’s Skills Different?

Section titled “What Makes Vercel’s Skills Different?”

Machine-Readable Format

Section titled “Machine-Readable Format”

Unlike traditional documentation that requires human interpretation, Vercel’s skills are structured specifically for LLM consumption. Rules are categorized by impact level and include clear trigger conditions.

Production-Tested Patterns

Section titled “Production-Tested Patterns”

These aren’t theoretical guidelines—they’re distilled from real-world experience building and optimizing some of the web’s largest React applications at Vercel scale.

Prioritized by Impact

Section titled “Prioritized by Impact”

Rules are tagged by priority (Critical, High, Medium, Low), helping agents focus on changes that provide the most value first.

Comprehensive Coverage

Section titled “Comprehensive Coverage”

With 100+ rules across web design and 40+ rules for React optimization, the skills cover edge cases that general AI training might miss.

Related Resources

Section titled “Related Resources”
  • GitHub Repository: vercel-labs/agent-skills
  • Agent Skills Format: agentskills.io
  • Vercel Blog: Introducing React Best Practices
  • DeepWiki: Skill Documentation

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Vercel Agent Skills represent a significant step toward making AI coding assistants more practical for production development. By encoding engineering best practices in machine-readable formats, Vercel has created a bridge between human expertise and AI capability.

Whether you’re building a React app, optimizing performance, auditing accessibility, or deploying to production, these skills provide your AI assistant with the context it needs to deliver high-quality, production-ready code.

The skills are open source (MIT License) and continuously evolving. As AI coding assistants become more capable, expect to see more skills covering additional frameworks, languages, and specialized domains.

Tags: #AI #Vercel #React #AgentSkills #DeveloperTools

Tags:
  • AI Agents
  • Vercel
  • React
  • Developer Tools
  • Skills
  • Open Source

Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: Kernel-Level Isolation for AI Agents

Jan 26, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: Kernel-Level Isolation for AI Agents

Section titled “Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: Kernel-Level Isolation for AI Agents”

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

AI coding assistants have transformed how developers work, but they’ve also introduced a new category of risk: what happens when an AI agent executes a terminal command that affects your system beyond the project directory?

Google Antigravity’s new Terminal Sandboxing feature addresses this head-on with kernel-level isolation. When enabled, all terminal commands executed by the AI agent are restricted to a designated workspace, preventing accidental modifications to system files, user directories, or other sensitive locations.

This is a significant step forward for AI agent safety, particularly as these tools gain more autonomy and the ability to run complex multi-step operations.

Key Highlights

Section titled “Key Highlights”
  • Kernel-level isolation via macOS Seatbelt (sandbox-exec) for robust security boundaries
  • File system restrictions limiting writes to workspace directory and essential system locations only
  • Independent network control — allow or deny network access while maintaining file system restrictions
  • Per-command bypass option in “Request Review” mode for flexibility when needed
  • Automatic activation in Secure Mode with network access denied by default
  • Linux support coming soon (currently macOS-only)

Prerequisites & Installation

Section titled “Prerequisites & Installation”

Requirements

Section titled “Requirements”
  • Google Antigravity (public preview — free account required)
  • macOS (current platform availability)
  • Antigravity desktop app or browser extension

Enabling Sandboxing

Section titled “Enabling Sandboxing”

Sandboxing is disabled by default but can be toggled in Antigravity User Settings:

  1. Open Antigravity Settings
  2. Find the Terminal section
  3. Toggle “Enable Terminal Sandboxing” to turn sandboxing on or off
  4. Optionally toggle “Sandbox Allow Network” to control network access independently

When Secure Mode is enabled, sandboxing activates automatically with network access denied for maximum protection.

How Sandboxing Works

Section titled “How Sandboxing Works”

Under the Hood: Seatbelt

Section titled “Under the Hood: Seatbelt”

Google Antigravity leverages Apple’s Seatbelt sandboxing mechanism (sandbox-exec), which provides kernel-level isolation for processes. This is the same technology macOS uses to sandbox apps from the App Store.

When sandboxing is enabled:

  1. File System: The agent can only write to the designated workspace directory and read from essential system locations
  2. Network Access: Controlled independently via the “Sandbox Allow Network” toggle
  3. Violations: Any operation outside permitted boundaries is immediately blocked with a clear error message

What Gets Blocked

Section titled “What Gets Blocked”

When an AI agent attempts an operation outside sandbox boundaries, users see explicit feedback:

Common blocked operations include:

  • Writing to directories outside the workspace
  • Deleting system files
  • Modifying user configuration files outside the project
  • Network requests (if network access is disabled)

Recommended Scenarios & Best Practices

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios & Best Practices”

For Individual Developers

Section titled “For Individual Developers”

Enable sandboxing when:

  • Working on critical systems where accidental changes could be costly
  • Letting AI agents run autonomously on complex tasks
  • Experimenting with new AI capabilities you don’t fully trust yet

Disable sandboxing when:

  • You need the agent to modify files across multiple project directories
  • Running trusted, well-understood commands
  • The friction of constant bypasses outweighs security benefits

For Teams and Enterprises

Section titled “For Teams and Enterprises”

Recommended Configuration:

  • Enable Secure Mode for team environments
  • Keep sandboxing ON with network access DENIED by default
  • Use “Request Review” mode with per-command bypass for exceptions
  • Document approved bypass scenarios in team guidelines

Daily Workflow Examples

Section titled “Daily Workflow Examples”

Scenario 1: Autonomous Refactoring

Scenario 2: Multi-Project Setup

Precautions & Troubleshooting

Section titled “Precautions & Troubleshooting”

Common Issues

Section titled “Common Issues”

Issue: Commands that previously worked now fail

Solution: Check if the command tries to access files outside the workspace. Common culprits:

  • Package managers installing to global directories
  • Build tools that cache outside the project
  • Configuration files in ~/.config or similar

Issue: Agent terminates unexpectedly

Solution: Some workflows may break when sandboxed. Review the agent’s error logs for sandbox-related messages and adjust the workspace scope or use per-command bypass.

Issue: Network operations fail

Solution: If the agent needs network access (e.g., downloading dependencies), enable “Sandbox Allow Network” in settings while keeping file system restrictions.

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: Is sandboxing enabled by default?

A: No, sandboxing is currently opt-in, but this may change in future releases. Google Antigravity suggests that defaults may evolve as the feature matures.

Q: Can I run commands outside the sandbox?

A: Yes. In “Request Review” mode, you’ll see a “Bypass Sandbox” option for individual commands. This gives you flexibility without permanently disabling protection.

Q: Does this work on Linux or Windows?

A: Currently macOS-only due to the use of Apple’s Seatbelt mechanism. Linux support is explicitly listed as “coming soon.” Windows support details are not yet available.

Q: How does this compare to Docker or VMs?

A: Sandboxing is lighter-weight than full containerization. It’s a security boundary for terminal commands specifically, not a full environment isolation solution like Docker.

Comparison with Similar Products

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Products”
FeatureGoogle AntigravityClaude CodeCursor
Terminal Sandboxing✅ Kernel-level (Seatbelt)❌ (planned)❌
File Operation Guard✅ Built-in✅ Built-in✅ Built-in
Network Control✅ Independent toggle⚠️ Partial❌
Per-Command Bypass✅ Request Review mode❌❌
Linux Support⚠️ Coming soon✅✅
macOS Support✅✅✅
Windows Support⚠️ TBD✅✅

Google Antigravity is the first major AI coding assistant to implement kernel-level terminal sandboxing, setting a new standard for AI agent safety.

User Reactions

Section titled “User Reactions”

The community response has been polarized:

Positive:

“Fantastic addition. You’re raising the bar every single day.”

Skeptical:

“I’ll stick with sudo gemini --yolo mode. Can we trust the models a bit more?”

Pragmatic:

“The important thing isn’t setting guardrails—it’s the model knowing what to run and where. But models aren’t perfect yet, so sandboxing is a necessary safety net.”

Some users have reported increased agent terminations after enabling sandboxing, suggesting that tighter security boundaries can expose fragile agent workflows.

Technical Deep Dive

Section titled “Technical Deep Dive”

Secure Mode Integration

Section titled “Secure Mode Integration”

When Secure Mode is enabled, the following configuration is automatically applied:

This configuration is designed for enterprise environments where data exfiltration and system modification are primary concerns.

Seatbelt Profile

Section titled “Seatbelt Profile”

While Google hasn’t published the exact Seatbelt profile being used, typical sandbox profiles include:

Future Directions

Section titled “Future Directions”

Given that Linux support is “coming soon,” Google Antigravity is likely implementing a cross-platform abstraction layer that will use:

  • Linux: Firejail, Bubblewrap, or seccomp-bpf
  • Windows: Windows Sandbox or AppContainer

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Official Documentation: Sandboxing Terminal Commands
  • Google Antigravity: https://antigravity.google
  • Apple Seatbelt: macOS Sandbox Guide
  • Community Discussion: Hacker News

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Google Antigravity’s terminal sandboxing represents a maturing of AI agent tooling. As AI assistants gain more capability, the need for robust safety boundaries becomes more pressing. Kernel-level isolation via Seatbelt provides exactly that—a security layer that operates below the application level, where even a misbehaving AI model cannot bypass it.

The feature is currently opt-in, but the trajectory is clear: as AI agents become more autonomous, sandboxing and similar safety mechanisms will move from optional to essential. For teams and individual developers alike, the question isn’t whether you need sandboxing—it’s whether you can afford not to have it.

Published: January 26, 2026

Tags:
  • Google Antigravity
  • AI Security
  • Terminal Sandboxing
  • macOS
  • Seatbelt
  • AI Agents
  • Kernel-Level Isolation

Dev Browser: State-ful Browser Automation for Claude Code

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Dev Browser: State-ful Browser Automation for Claude Code

Section titled “Dev Browser: State-ful Browser Automation for Claude Code”

As AI agents become increasingly capable of writing code, the next logical step is giving them the ability to interact with real-world web pages. Dev Browser is an open-source Skill for Claude Code that does exactly this—while keeping all the state of your browser alive between calls.

Why Dev Browser Matters

Section titled “Why Dev Browser Matters”

Dev Browser addresses a critical gap in AI agent capabilities: persistent browser automation. Traditional browser automation tools either execute actions one at a time (slow) or run full scripts that start fresh every time (fragile). Dev Browser takes a different approach.

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”
  • Persistent pages – Navigate to a page once and keep it in memory. No need to re-load the DOM for every script you run.
  • Flexible execution – Either run a full script in one go or step through actions when you’re exploring.
  • LLM-friendly DOM snapshots – Structured, lightweight representations of the page that Claude can parse quickly.
  • Chrome extension – Control an existing Chrome profile to leverage logged-in sessions, cookies, and extensions.

These features give Claude Code agents a practical edge for testing, data extraction, and interactive demos.

Quick Start Guide

Section titled “Quick Start Guide”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”
  • Claude Code CLI installed
  • Node.js (v18 or later) with npm

Installation

Section titled “Installation”

Method 1: Claude Code Marketplace (Recommended)

Section titled “Method 1: Claude Code Marketplace (Recommended)”

Restart Claude Code after installation.

Method 2: Manual Installation

Section titled “Method 2: Manual Installation”

Amp only: Start the server manually before use:

Chrome Extension (Optional but Powerful)

Section titled “Chrome Extension (Optional but Powerful)”

The Chrome extension allows Dev Browser to control your existing Chrome browser instead of launching a separate Chromium instance. This gives you access to your logged-in sessions, bookmarks, and extensions.

Installation:

  1. Download extension.zip from the latest release
  2. Unzip the file to a permanent location (e.g., ~/.dev-browser-extension)
  3. Open Chrome and go to chrome://extensions
  4. Enable “Developer mode” (toggle in top right)
  5. Click “Load unpacked” and select the unzipped extension folder

Using the extension:

  1. Click the Dev Browser extension icon in Chrome’s toolbar
  2. Toggle it to “Active” - this enables browser control
  3. Ask Claude to connect to your browser (e.g., “connect to my Chrome” or “use the extension”)

When active, Claude can control your existing Chrome tabs with all your logged-in sessions, cookies, and extensions intact.

Permissions Configuration

Section titled “Permissions Configuration”

To skip permission prompts, add to ~/.claude/settings.json:

Or run with claude --dangerously-skip-permissions (skips all prompts).

Usage Examples

Section titled “Usage Examples”

Just ask Claude to interact with your browser:

“Open localhost:3000 and verify the signup flow works”

“Go to the settings page and figure out why the save button isn’t working”

“Navigate to my dashboard and take a screenshot of the analytics chart”

Performance Benchmarks

Section titled “Performance Benchmarks”

Dev Browser isn’t just another browser automation tool—it’s significantly faster and more reliable than alternatives:

MethodTimeCostTurnsSuccess
Dev Browser3m 53s$0.8829100%
Playwright MCP4m 31s$1.4551100%
Playwright Skill8m 07s$1.453867%
Claude Chrome Extension12m 54s$2.8180100%

See dev-browser-eval for methodology.

How It’s Different

Section titled “How It’s Different”
ApproachHow It WorksTradeoff
Playwright MCPObserve-think-act loop with individual tool callsSimple but slow; each action is a separate round-trip
Playwright SkillFull scripts that run end-to-endFast but fragile; scripts start fresh every time
Dev BrowserStateful server + agentic script executionBest of both: persistent state with flexible execution

Technical Architecture

Section titled “Technical Architecture”

Dev Browser’s innovation lies in its hybrid architecture:

  1. Stateful Server: Maintains browser pages in memory between operations
  2. Agentic Execution: Can run full scripts when confident, fall back to step-by-step when exploring
  3. Optimized DOM Snapshots: Page representations are structured for AI consumption, not just raw HTML

This design makes it ideal for:

  • E2E Testing: Verify your application works end-to-end
  • Data Extraction: Scrape web content with intelligent parsing
  • Debugging: Have Claude investigate UI issues interactively
  • Automated Workflows: Log into sites and perform multi-step actions

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Start Simple

Section titled “1. Start Simple”

Begin with straightforward tasks like navigation and screenshots before attempting complex workflows.

2. Use Chrome Extension for Auth

Section titled “2. Use Chrome Extension for Auth”

For sites requiring login, use the Chrome extension to maintain your session.

3. Provide Clear Context

Section titled “3. Provide Clear Context”

Give Claude specific instructions about what you’re looking for or trying to accomplish.

4. Handle Dynamic Content

Section titled “4. Handle Dynamic Content”

Be aware that Dev Browser works best with pages that have stable DOM structures. Highly dynamic content may require additional guidance.

Limitations and Considerations

Section titled “Limitations and Considerations”
  • Single Browser Instance: Dev Browser maintains one browser instance at a time
  • Chrome Extension Required for Sessions: To use existing logged-in sessions, you need the Chrome extension
  • Learning Curve: Like any tool, it takes practice to craft effective prompts

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”

vs. Playwright MCP

Section titled “vs. Playwright MCP”

Playwright MCP uses a traditional observe-think-act loop where each browser action requires a separate API call. This is simple but results in many round-trips. Dev Browser batches operations intelligently.

vs. Selenium

Section titled “vs. Selenium”

Selenium is a mature browser automation framework but lacks AI-optimized features. Dev Browser is specifically designed for LLM consumption with structured page representations.

vs. Puppeteer

Section titled “vs. Puppeteer”

Puppeteer is similar to Playwright but Chrome-only. Dev Browser uses Playwright under the hood for cross-browser support.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Dev Browser represents a significant step forward in AI agent capabilities. By combining persistent browser state with intelligent execution modes, it enables Claude Code to interact with web pages in ways that are both faster and more reliable than previous approaches.

Whether you’re automating tests, extracting data, or debugging UI issues, Dev Browser provides a practical solution that bridges the gap between AI code generation and real-world web interaction.

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • GitHub Repository
  • npm Package
  • Claude Code Documentation
  • Evaluation Benchmarks

License

Section titled “License”

MIT License - freely usable for personal and commercial projects.

Author

Section titled “Author”

Sawyer Hood

Tags:
  • claude-code
  • browser-automation
  • ai-agents
  • playwright
  • developer-tools

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

In the cybersecurity field, Trail of Bits has long been a respected security research and consulting company. Now, they have launched a professional skills marketplace for Claude Code, packaging years of security audit experience into 13+ ready-to-use AI skill plugins. This marks a new era in AI-assisted security auditing.

What is Trail of Bits Skills?

Section titled “What is Trail of Bits Skills?”

Trail of Bits Skills is an open-source Claude Code plugin marketplace that provides specialized skills for security research, vulnerability detection, and audit workflows.

Key Skills

Section titled “Smart Contract Security”
  • Solidity security analysis
  • DeFi vulnerability detection
  • Contract upgrade safety checks

Static Analysis

  • Vulnerability scanning

Claude Skills: 48 Production-Ready Skills for Claude Code and Claude AI

Jan 16, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

As AI coding assistants and AI agents become more capable, developers and teams are looking for ways to customize and extend these tools with domain-specific expertise. Enter claude-skills - a comprehensive collection of 48 production-ready skill packages designed to augment Claude AI and Claude Code with specialized professional knowledge across multiple domains.

What is Claude Skills?

Section titled “What is Claude Skills?”

claude-skills by Alireza Rezvani is an open-source repository providing modular, self-contained skill packages for Claude AI and Claude Code. Each skill package includes:

  • 📖 Comprehensive documentation - Workflows, best practices, and strategic frameworks
  • 🛠️ Python analysis tools - 68+ CLI utilities for automated analysis and optimization
  • 📚 Knowledge bases - Curated reference materials and guidelines
  • 📋 Ready-to-use templates - Customizable assets for immediate deployment

Project Statistics

Section titled “Project Statistics”

With 718 GitHub stars and 126 forks, the project has gained significant traction in the AI development community. The repository features:

  • 48 production-ready skills
  • 6 major skill categories
  • 68+ Python CLI tools
  • MIT License for commercial use

Installation Methods

Section titled “Installation Methods”

Method 1: Claude Code Plugin System

Section titled “Method 1: Claude Code Plugin System”

Use Claude Code’s built-in plugin system for native integration:

Method 2: Universal Installer

Section titled “Method 2: Universal Installer”

Install to all supported AI agents simultaneously:

Supported Agents: Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code, Copilot, Goose, Amp, Codex, Letta, OpenCode

Skill Categories

Section titled “Skill Categories”

1. Marketing Skills (5 skills)

Section titled “1. Marketing Skills (5 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

  • Content Creator - Brand voice analysis, SEO optimization, content frameworks
  • Marketing Demand Acquisition - CAC calculator, full-funnel strategy, channel playbooks
  • Product Marketing - Positioning, GTM strategy, competitive intelligence
  • App Store Optimization - ASO toolkit for Apple App Store and Google Play
  • Social Media Analyzer - Campaign analytics across platforms

2. C-Level Advisory Skills (2 skills)

Section titled “2. C-Level Advisory Skills (2 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

  • CEO Advisor - Strategic planning, financial modeling, board governance
  • CTO Advisor - Technical debt analysis, team scaling, architecture decisions

3. Product Team Skills (5 skills)

Section titled “3. Product Team Skills (5 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

  • Product Manager Toolkit - RICE prioritization, interview analysis, PRD templates
  • Agile Product Owner - User story generation, sprint planning, velocity tracking
  • Product Strategist - OKR cascading, strategic planning, vision frameworks
  • UX Researcher Designer - Persona generation, journey mapping, research synthesis
  • UI Design System - Design tokens, component architecture, system documentation

4. Project Management Skills (6 skills)

Section titled “4. Project Management Skills (6 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

  • Senior PM Expert - Portfolio management, stakeholder alignment
  • Scrum Master Expert - Sprint ceremonies, agile coaching
  • Jira Expert - JQL mastery, configuration, automation
  • Confluence Expert - Knowledge management, documentation
  • Atlassian Administrator - System administration, security
  • Template Creator - Template design and standardization

5. Engineering Team Skills (18 skills)

Section titled “5. Engineering Team Skills (18 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

Core Engineering:

  • Senior Software Architect - Architecture design, tech decisions
  • Senior Frontend Engineer - React/Next.js development
  • Senior Backend Engineer - API design, database optimization
  • Senior Fullstack Engineer - End-to-end development
  • Senior QA Testing Engineer - Test automation, coverage analysis
  • Senior DevOps Engineer - CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure as code
  • Senior SecOps Engineer - Security operations, vulnerability management
  • Code Reviewer - PR analysis, code quality
  • Senior Security Engineer - Security architecture, penetration testing
  • AWS Solution Architect - Serverless architectures, cost optimization
  • Microsoft 365 Tenant Manager - Tenant configuration, automation
  • TDD Guide - Test-driven development methodology
  • Tech Stack Evaluator - Technology evaluation, vendor selection

AI/ML/Data (5 skills):

  • Senior Data Scientist - Statistical modeling, experimentation
  • Senior Data Engineer - Data pipelines, ETL/ELT
  • Senior ML/AI Engineer - MLOps, model deployment, LLM integration
  • Senior Prompt Engineer - LLM optimization, RAG systems
  • Senior Computer Vision Engineer - Object detection, image/video AI

6. Regulatory Affairs & Quality Management (12 skills)

Section titled “6. Regulatory Affairs & Quality Management (12 skills)”

Status: ✅ Production Ready

  • Senior Regulatory Affairs Manager - Strategic regulatory leadership
  • Senior Quality Manager (QMR) - Overall quality system responsibility
  • Senior QMS ISO 13485 Specialist - QMS implementation
  • Senior CAPA Officer - Corrective/preventive action management
  • Senior Quality Documentation Manager - Regulatory documentation control
  • Senior Risk Management Specialist - ISO 14971 risk management
  • Senior Information Security Manager - ISO 27001 ISMS
  • Senior MDR 2017/745 Specialist - EU MDR compliance
  • Senior FDA Consultant - FDA pathways and QSR compliance
  • Senior QMS Audit Expert - Internal and external auditing
  • Senior ISMS Audit Expert - Security system auditing
  • Senior GDPR/DSGVO Expert - Privacy and data protection

Example Usage

Section titled “Example Usage”

Content Creator Skill

Section titled “Content Creator Skill”

Fullstack Engineer Skill

Section titled “Fullstack Engineer Skill”

Benefits

Section titled “Benefits”

Key Advantages:

  • ⚡ Immediate deployment - Download and use in minutes
  • 🎯 Domain expertise - Battle-tested frameworks from industry experts
  • 🔧 Practical tools - Algorithmic analysis without external API dependencies
  • 📈 Measurable ROI - 40%+ time savings, 30%+ quality improvements

Time Savings Per Organization:

  • Marketing teams: 310 hours/month
  • C-level executives: 30 hours/month
  • Product teams: 180 hours/month

Related Projects

The claude-skills repository is part of a larger ecosystem:

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

claude-skills represents a significant step forward in democratizing professional expertise through AI. With 48 production-ready skills across 6 major categories, it provides teams and individuals with powerful tools to augment their AI workflows.

Whether you’re a marketer, engineer, product manager, or executive, there’s likely a skill package that can help you work smarter and faster with Claude AI and Claude Code.

Links
