Expo Skills: Official Expo AI Agent Skills Repository Deep Dive

Jan 16, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

In 2026, as AI agents and Claude Code gain widespread adoption, more development teams are creating specialized “skill packages” for AI assistants. The Expo team has led the way by releasing the official expo/skills repository — a collection of AI agent skills specifically designed for Expo and React Native development.

What is Expo Skills?

Section titled “What is Expo Skills?”

expo/skills is the official AI agent skills repository maintained by the Expo team, designed for building, deploying, and debugging Expo applications. These skills have been validated through internal use at Expo and are fine-tuned for Claude Opus models, but remain compatible with any AI agent.

Project Overview:

  • Repository: https://github.com/expo/skills
  • Stars: 149+
  • Forks: 6+
  • License: MIT
  • Language: JavaScript

Installation Methods

Section titled “Installation Methods”

For Claude Code Users (Recommended)

Section titled “For Claude Code Users (Recommended)”

Expo team primarily uses Claude Code, making this the optimal experience:

For Any AI Agent

Section titled “For Any AI Agent”

Note: This approach extracts skills individually, requiring manual update management.

Core Skill Plugins

Section titled “Core Skill Plugins”

1. Expo App Design

Section titled “1. Expo App Design”

Capabilities:

  • UI guidelines following Apple Human Interface Guidelines
  • Expo Router navigation patterns (stacks, tabs, modals, sheets)
  • Native iOS controls, SF Symbols, animations, and visual effects
  • API route creation with EAS Hosting
  • Data fetching patterns with React Query and offline support
  • Tailwind CSS v4 with NativeWind v5 setup
  • DOM components for running web code in native apps

When to Use:

  • Building new Expo apps from scratch
  • Adding navigation, styling, or animations
  • Setting up API routes or data fetching
  • Integrating web libraries via DOM components
  • Configuring Tailwind CSS for React Native

Included Skills:

  • building-ui — UI components, navigation, styling, native controls
  • api-routes — Server-side API routes with EAS Hosting
  • data-fetching — Network requests, caching, offline support
  • dev-client — Development builds and TestFlight distribution
  • tailwind-setup — Tailwind CSS v4 configuration
  • use-dom — Web code integration via DOM components

2. Upgrading Expo

Section titled “2. Upgrading Expo”

Capabilities:

  • Step-by-step upgrade process guidance (npx expo install expo@latest)
  • Identifies deprecated packages and modern replacements
  • Cleans up outdated configuration (babel, metro, postcss)
  • Handles cache clearing for both managed and bare workflows
  • Knowledge of breaking changes between SDK versions

When to Use:

  • Upgrading to a new Expo SDK version
  • Fixing dependency conflicts after an upgrade
  • Migrating from deprecated packages (expo-av → expo-audio/expo-video)
  • Cleaning up legacy configuration files

3. Expo Deployment

Section titled “3. Expo Deployment”

Capabilities:

  • Guides iOS App Store and TestFlight submissions
  • Covers Android Play Store deployment and track management
  • Explains EAS Build configuration and version management
  • Helps write and validate EAS Workflow YAML files for CI/CD
  • Web deployment with EAS Hosting

When to Use:

  • Submitting apps to App Store Connect or Google Play
  • Setting up TestFlight beta testing
  • Configuring EAS Build profiles
  • Writing CI/CD workflows for automated deployments
  • Deploying web apps with EAS Hosting

Included Skills:

  • deployment — Store submissions, EAS Build, and web hosting
  • cicd-workflows — EAS Workflow YAML files for CI/CD automation

4. EAS CI/CD Workflows

Section titled “4. EAS CI/CD Workflows”

Automated workflow configuration skills for continuous integration and deployment, helping developers set up complete automated deployment pipelines.

The AI Agent Skills Ecosystem

Section titled “The AI Agent Skills Ecosystem”

Expo Skills is part of the broader AI agent skills ecosystem. Similar to SkillMaster.cc, AI Agent Skills, and Cursor Agent Skills, Expo Skills represents the trend of packaging domain-specific expertise for AI assistants.

Comparison with Other Skill Platforms:

PlatformFocusCharacteristics
Expo SkillsExpo/React NativeOfficially maintained, mobile-focused
SkillMasterGeneral SkillsMulti-platform, curated skills
Cursor Agent SkillsCode EditorsIDE-integrated, development workflow
Claude Code SkillsClaude EcosystemOpen standard, community-driven

Best Practices and Usage Tips

Section titled “Best Practices and Usage Tips”

1. Choose the Right Model

Section titled “1. Choose the Right Model”

Expo team primarily uses Claude Opus, and skills are fine-tuned for this model. Use Opus for the best experience.

2. Install Skills as Needed

Section titled “2. Install Skills as Needed”

Only install skill plugins relevant to your current project to avoid unnecessary context distraction.

3. Combine with Official Documentation

Section titled “3. Combine with Official Documentation”

AI skills complement, not replace, official documentation. For complex issues, refer to Expo’s official docs.

4. Community Contributions

Section titled “4. Community Contributions”

Expo Skills is MIT-licensed and welcomes community contributions. See CONTRIBUTING.md for guidelines.

Technical Architecture

Section titled “Technical Architecture”

Future Outlook

Section titled “Future Outlook”

As AI agent technology matures, we can expect:

  1. More official skill repositories from major frameworks (Next.js, Vue, Django, etc.)
  2. Skill standard unification — Cross-platform skill format standards may emerge
  3. Maturing ecosystem — npm-like skill package managers
  4. Enterprise skill marketplaces — Private internal skill repositories for enterprises

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Expo Skills Repo: https://github.com/expo/skills
  • Expo Documentation: https://docs.expo.dev/
  • Claude Code: https://claude.com/claude-code
  • SkillMaster Marketplace: https://skillmaster.cc/
  • AI Agent Skills: https://www.aiagentskills.ai/

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

The release of Expo Skills marks an important milestone in the AI agent skills ecosystem. As the first official AI skills repository from a major framework, it sets an example for other open-source projects while providing developers with high-quality AI-assisted development tools. As this trend continues, we expect to see more frameworks and tech stacks launch their own official skill repositories.

Tags:
  • Expo
  • React Native
  • Claude Code
  • AI Agents
  • Mobile Development
  • Skill Packages

React Best Practices: Vercel's Performance Framework for AI Agents

Jan 16, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Performance work in React is typically reactive - a release goes out, users report slowness, and teams chase symptoms. Vercel has distilled over a decade of React and Next.js optimization experience into react-best-practices, a structured framework designed specifically for AI agents and development teams.

This framework addresses the most common root causes found in production codebases:

  • Async operations that accidentally become sequential
  • Large client bundles that grow over time
  • Components that re-render unnecessarily

Why Performance Work Fails

Section titled “Why Performance Work Fails”

Most performance optimization fails because it starts too low in the stack. If a request waterfall adds 600ms of waiting time, optimizing useMemo calls won’t matter. If you ship an extra 300KB of JavaScript on every page, micro-optimizing loops won’t help.

Performance work compounds. A small regression shipped today becomes a long-term tax on every user session until someone pays down the debt.

The Framework: 8 Categories, 40+ Rules

Section titled “The Framework: 8 Categories, 40+ Rules”

The react-best-practices framework is ordered by impact, from CRITICAL (eliminating waterfalls, reducing bundle size) to incremental optimizations:

1. Eliminating Async Waterfalls

Section titled “1. Eliminating Async Waterfalls”

Impact: CRITICAL

Request waterfalls are among the most expensive performance problems. Consider this common anti-pattern:

2. Bundle Size Optimization

Section titled “2. Bundle Size Optimization”

Impact: CRITICAL

  • Reduce initial JavaScript payload
  • Implement code splitting strategically
  • Remove unused dependencies

3. Server-Side Performance

Section titled “3. Server-Side Performance”

Server components, streaming, and parallel data loading

4. Client-Side Data Fetching

Section titled “4. Client-Side Data Fetching”

Optimizing useEffect chains, preventing cascading requests

5. Re-render Optimization

Section titled “5. Re-render Optimization”

Memoization strategies and preventing unnecessary renders

6. Rendering Performance

Section titled “6. Rendering Performance”

Virtualization, windowing, and expensive operations

7. Advanced Patterns

Section titled “7. Advanced Patterns”

Suspense, concurrent features, and edge cases

8. JavaScript Performance

Section titled “8. JavaScript Performance”

Loop optimization, memory management, and micro-optimizations

Real-World Impact

Section titled “Real-World Impact”

These aren’t theoretical practices - they come from actual production work:

Combining Loop Iterations A chat page was scanning the same list of messages eight separate times. Combining into a single pass significantly improved performance at scale.

Parallelizing Awaits An API waited for database calls sequentially when they could run in parallel. Running them concurrently cut wait time in half.

Tuning Font Fallbacks Headlines looked cramped when system fonts displayed before custom fonts loaded. Adjusting letter-spacing made the fallback look intentional.

AI Agent Integration

Section titled “AI Agent Integration”

The best practices are packaged as Agent Skills that install into:

  • Claude Code
  • Opencode
  • Codex
  • Cursor
  • Other coding agents

When your agent spots cascading useEffect calls or heavy client-side imports, it can reference these patterns and suggest fixes automatically.

How It Works

Section titled “How It Works”

Individual rule files compile into AGENTS.md - a single document your agents can query when:

  • Reviewing code
  • Suggesting optimizations
  • Performing refactors

This ensures consistent application of performance decisions across large codebases, whether by human developers or AI agents.

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

Visit the react-best-practices repository to:

  • Browse all 40+ rules
  • See code examples for each pattern
  • Install as an Agent Skill
  • Contribute your own patterns

Key Takeaways

Section titled “Key Takeaways”
  1. Start at the top - Fix waterfalls and bundle size before micro-optimizations
  2. Performance compounds - Small regressions become long-term taxes
  3. Consistency matters - Use frameworks, not ad-hoc decisions
  4. AI-friendly - Structured for agents to reference and apply

References

Section titled “References”
  • React Best Practices Repository
  • Agent Skills Collection
  • Vercel Blog Announcement
Tags:
  • react
  • performance
  • best-practices
  • vercel
  • ai-agents
  • optimization

UI Skills: Opinionated Constraints for Building Better Interfaces with AI Agents

Jan 16, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

As AI coding assistants become increasingly capable of building user interfaces, a new challenge has emerged: consistency. While agents can write functional code, they often produce interfaces with varying design patterns, inconsistent styling, and accessibility gaps. Enter UI Skills—a set of opinionated constraints designed to help agents ship better, more consistent interfaces.

What is UI Skills?

Section titled “What is UI Skills?”

UI Skills is a collection of opinionated UI constraints that serve as a guardrail for AI agents building user interfaces. When invoked, these constraints ensure that:

  • Interfaces follow consistent design patterns
  • Accessibility best practices are built-in
  • Performance is optimized by default
  • Code remains maintainable and scalable

The project supports multiple AI coding environments including Claude Code, Cursor, OpenCode, and others that accept skill definitions.

Why Opinionated Constraints Matter

Section titled “Why Opinionated Constraints Matter”

In the world of AI-generated UI, opinionated constraints serve several critical purposes:

1. Consistency Across Agents

Section titled “1. Consistency Across Agents”

Without constraints, different agents (or even the same agent at different times) might produce wildly different implementations. UI Skills establishes a single source of truth for design decisions.

2. Accessibility by Default

Section titled “2. Accessibility by Default”

Many AI-generated interfaces overlook accessibility. UI Skills mandates accessible patterns from the start, ensuring compliance with WCAG standards without manual intervention.

3. Performance Optimization

Section titled “3. Performance Optimization”

Agents often choose the easiest implementation path, not the most performant one. UI Skills constrains choices to proven performance patterns.

Installation and Usage

Section titled “Installation and Usage”

Basic Installation

Section titled “Basic Installation”

UI Skills can be integrated into your AI coding workflow:

Invoking the Skill

Section titled “Invoking the Skill”

Once installed, invoke UI Skills in your AI coding session:

  • /ui-skills — Apply constraints to any UI work in the current conversation
  • /ui-skills <file> — Review a specific file against all constraints

File Review Output

Section titled “File Review Output”

When reviewing a file, UI Skills provides:

  • Violations: Exact line/snippet quotes of problematic code
  • Why it matters: One-sentence explanation of each issue
  • Concrete fix: Code-level suggestion for resolution

Technology Stack Requirements

Section titled “Technology Stack Requirements”

UI Skills enforces specific technology choices to ensure consistency:

Mandatory Technologies

Section titled “Mandatory Technologies”
  • Tailwind CSS: Default utility-first CSS framework
  • motion/react (formerly framer-motion): JavaScript animations
  • tw-animate-css: CSS-based entrance and micro-animations
  • cn utility: clsx + tailwind-merge for class logic

Component Primitives

Section titled “Component Primitives”

For components with keyboard or focus behavior, UI Skills mandates:

  • Accessible primitives: Base UI, React Aria, or Radix
  • Project primitives first: Use existing components before adding new ones
  • No primitive mixing: Never combine different primitive systems in one interaction surface
  • Base UI preference: For new projects, prefer Base UI components

Core Constraint Categories

Section titled “Core Constraint Categories”

1. Components

Section titled “1. Components”

UI Skills enforces these component-level rules:

RuleRationale
Add aria-label to icon-only buttonsScreen reader accessibility
Use AlertDialog for destructive actionsPrevents accidental data loss
Use structural skeletons for loadingPerceived performance improvement
Never use h-screen, use h-dvhMobile viewport compatibility
Respect safe-area-inset for fixed elementsNotch/device compatibility
Show errors next to actionBetter user feedback
Never block paste in inputsUser experience enhancement

2. Animation

Section titled “2. Animation”

Animation constraints focus on performance and UX:

RuleRationale
Never animate unless explicitly requestedReduces motion sickness
Animate only compositor props (transform, opacity)60fps performance
Never animate layout propertiesExpensive reflows
Avoid animating paint properties (background, color)GPU optimization
Use ease-out on entranceNatural motion feel
Never exceed 200ms for interaction feedbackResponsive feel
Pause looping animations off-screenBattery/resource conservation
Respect prefers-reduced-motionAccessibility compliance
Avoid animating large imagesPerformance optimization

3. Typography

Section titled “3. Typography”

Typography constraints ensure readability:

RuleRationale
Use text-balance for headingsImproved visual centering
Use text-pretty for body/paragraphsBetter line breaking
Use tabular-nums for dataPrevents layout shift
Use truncate/line-clamp for dense UIContent fit
Never modify letter-spacing unless requestedFont designer intent

4. Layout

Section titled “4. Layout”

Layout rules maintain z-index sanity:

RuleRationale
Use fixed z-index scalePrevents z-index wars
Use size-* for square elementsCode conciseness

5. Performance

Section titled “5. Performance”

Performance constraints prevent common anti-patterns:

RuleRationale
Never animate large blur() surfacesGPU memory
Never apply will-change outside active animationBrowser optimization
Never use useEffect for render logicPrevents unnecessary renders

6. Design

Section titled “6. Design”

Design constraints ensure visual consistency:

RuleRationale
Never use gradients unless requestedCleaner aesthetic
Never use purple/multicolor gradientsTrend avoidance
Never use glow effects as primary affordancesAccessibility
Use Tailwind default shadows unless requestedConsistency
Give empty states one clear next actionUser guidance
Limit accent color to one per viewVisual hierarchy
Use existing theme colors before new onesDesign system integrity

Recommended Scenarios

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios”

When to Use UI Skills

Section titled “When to Use UI Skills”
  1. AI-Generated UI Development: When Claude Code or similar agents build interfaces
  2. Code Reviews: Automated validation of UI code quality
  3. Team Onboarding: Teaching consistent UI patterns to developers
  4. Design System Alignment: Ensuring implementations match design tokens
  5. Accessibility Audits: Catching a11y issues before production

Daily Workflow Integration

Section titled “Daily Workflow Integration”

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

For Developers

Section titled “For Developers”
  • Invoke early: Apply UI Skills at the start of UI work, not as an afterthought
  • Review iteratively: Check files as you build, not just at the end
  • Understand the why: Each constraint exists for a reason—learn them
  • Customize thoughtfully: If you must break a rule, understand the trade-off

For Teams

Section titled “For Teams”
  • Standardize: Make UI Skills part of your team’s AI workflow
  • Document additions: Extend constraints for team-specific patterns
  • Review together: Use constraint violations as learning opportunities

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”

UI Skills vs. Linters

Section titled “UI Skills vs. Linters”
FeatureUI SkillsTraditional Linters
AI-native✅ Designed for agent workflows❌ General-purpose
Opinionated✅ Strong prescriptive constraints⚠️ Often configurable to permissiveness
Context-aware✅ Understands UI/UX trade-offs❌ Syntax-focused
Educational✅ Explains why each rule matters⚠️ Often rule-reference only

UI Skills vs. Design Systems

Section titled “UI Skills vs. Design Systems”
FeatureUI SkillsDesign Systems
Component library❌ No components provided✅ Full component library
Constraints✅ Behavioral + visual⚠️ Primarily visual
AI-optimized✅ Agent workflow integration❌ Human-focused
Implementation-agnostic✅ Works with any stack⚠️ Often framework-specific

Precautions and Limitations

Section titled “Precautions and Limitations”

What UI Skills Doesn’t Do

Section titled “What UI Skills Doesn’t Do”
  • Provide components: It’s a ruleset, not a component library
  • Replace design systems: Works alongside, not instead of
  • Cover all edge cases: You may still need custom rules for specific contexts

When to Customize

Section titled “When to Customize”

If your project has specific requirements that conflict with UI Skills constraints:

  1. Document the deviation with team consensus
  2. Extend the skill with project-specific rules
  3. Maintain the core philosophy even when breaking specific rules

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: Can I use UI Skills with any CSS framework?

A: UI Skills is optimized for Tailwind CSS. While the principles can apply to other frameworks, some constraints are Tailwind-specific.

Q: Does UI Skills replace accessibility testing?

A: No. UI Skills catches many common a11y issues, but comprehensive accessibility testing is still recommended.

Q: Can I extend UI Skills with custom rules?

A: Yes. The project is designed to be extended with your team’s specific constraints.

Q: Is UI Skills only for AI-generated code?

A: While optimized for AI workflows, human developers can benefit from the constraints as well—especially when reviewing AI-generated code.

Q: What if a constraint doesn’t fit my use case?

A: Understand the rationale behind the rule, document your exception, and consider whether there’s an alternative approach that satisfies both the constraint and your requirement.

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Common Issues

Section titled “Common Issues”

Issue: False positives on custom components

  • Solution: Extend the skill with project-specific patterns

Issue: Conflicts with existing design system

  • Solution: Map design system tokens to Tailwind equivalents, or customize constraints

Issue: Too many violations on legacy code

  • Solution: Address incrementally, starting with critical accessibility issues

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Official Website: https://www.ui-skills.com/
  • GitHub Repository: Check official site for repository link
  • Related Projects:
    • Tailwind CSS
    • motion (React)
    • Base UI
    • Radix Primitives

Summary: UI Skills provides the guardrails needed for AI agents to generate consistently high-quality, accessible, and performant user interfaces. By establishing opinionated constraints across components, animation, typography, layout, and performance, it transforms AI-generated UI from a wildcard into a reliable production asset.

Tags:
  • AI Agents
  • UI/UX
  • Claude Code
  • Frontend
  • Developer Tools

BMAD Architecture Deep Dive: 26 Agents, 68 Workflows, and 655 Files

Jan 15, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

BMAD (Business Method Agent Development) represents a paradigm shift in how we approach software development and project management. It’s not just another AI assistant—it’s a comprehensive multi-agent system with 26 specialized agents, 68 registered workflows, 4 core tasks, and 655 files working in harmony.

This article takes you deep into BMAD’s architecture, revealing how its modular design enables specialized expertise across business/IT projects, game development, creative innovation, and system building.

System Overview: Six Modules

Section titled “System Overview: Six Modules”

BMAD’s architecture is organized into six main modules, each serving a distinct purpose:

1. Agents: 26 Specialized Personas

Section titled “1. Agents: 26 Specialized Personas”

Core Module (1 Agent)

Section titled “Core Module (1 Agent)”

bmad-master - The master executor and workflow orchestrator that coordinates all other agents.

BMM Module: Business & IT (9 Agents)

Section titled “BMM Module: Business & IT (9 Agents)”

The Business Method Module contains agents for traditional software development:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
analystMaryBusiness analyst, market research, requirements
architectWinstonSystem architect, technical design
devAmeliaSenior software engineer, implementation
pmJohnProduct manager, PRD creation
smBobScrum master, agile ceremonies
teaMuratMaster test architect, quality assurance
tech-writerPaigeTechnical documentation specialist
ux-designerSallyUX/UI designer
quick-flow-solo-devBarryFull-stack developer with Quick Flow focus

BMB Module: System Builders (3 Agents)

Section titled “BMB Module: System Builders (3 Agents)”

For extending the BMAD platform itself:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
agent-builderBondAgent architecture specialist
module-builderMorganModule architecture specialist
workflow-builderWendyWorkflow architecture specialist

BMGD Module: Game Development (6 Agents)

Section titled “BMGD Module: Game Development (6 Agents)”

Specialized agents for game creation:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
game-architectCloud DragonbornGame systems architect
game-designerSamus ShepardLead game designer
game-devLink FreemanGame developer
game-qaGLaDOSGame QA architect
game-scrum-masterMaxGame development scrum master
game-solo-devIndieElite indie game developer

CIS Module: Creative Innovation (6 Agents)

Section titled “CIS Module: Creative Innovation (6 Agents)”

For creativity and innovation:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
brainstorming-coachCarsonMaster brainstorming facilitator
creative-problem-solverDr. QuinnSystematic problem-solving expert
design-thinking-coachMayaHuman-centered design expert
innovation-strategistVictorBusiness model innovator
presentation-masterCaravaggioVisual communication expert
storytellerSophiaMaster storyteller

Agent Characteristics

Section titled “Agent Characteristics”

Each BMAD agent features:

  • Unique persona with distinct communication style
  • Domain expertise in specific areas
  • BMAD compliance standards
  • Menu-based interface for workflow execution
  • Multi-language support with configuration-driven communication

2. Workflows: 68 Registered + 481 Implementation Files

Section titled “2. Workflows: 68 Registered + 481 Implementation Files”

Core Workflows (2)

Section titled “Core Workflows (2)”
  • brainstorming - Interactive brainstorming sessions
  • party-mode - Multi-agent conversation orchestration

BMM Workflows: Business Method (22)

Section titled “BMM Workflows: Business Method (22)”

Covering the complete software development lifecycle:

Analysis Phase:

  • research - Comprehensive research across domains
  • create-product-brief - Product brief discovery

Planning Phase:

  • create-ux-design - UX patterns and design planning
  • prd - PRD creation, validation, and editing

Solutioning Phase:

  • create-architecture - Architecture decision facilitation
  • create-epics-and-stories - Transform PRDs into actionable stories
  • check-implementation-readiness - Pre-implementation validation

Implementation Phase:

  • code-review - Adversarial senior developer review
  • dev-story - Execute user stories with implementation
  • sprint-planning - Sprint status tracking
  • retrospective - Post-epic review and lessons learned

Quick Flow:

  • quick-dev - Flexible development execution
  • quick-spec - Conversational spec engineering

BMB Workflows: Builders (3)

Section titled “BMB Workflows: Builders (3)”
  • agent - Tri-modal agent creation/editing
  • module - Quad-modal module creation
  • workflow - Workflow creation architecture

BMGD Workflows: Game Development (29)

Section titled “BMGD Workflows: Game Development (29)”

The most comprehensive suite for game development:

Preproduction:

  • brainstorm-game - Game-specific brainstorming
  • create-game-brief - Game brief through discovery
  • game-brief - Game brief creation

Design:

  • create-gdd - Game Design Document creation
  • gdd - Game Design Document workflows
  • narrative - Narrative design workflows

Technical:

  • game-architecture - Game architecture workflow
  • generate-project-context - Project context generation

Production:

  • code-review - Game-specific code reviews
  • correct-course - Sprint course correction
  • dev-story - Game story execution
  • retrospective - Game development retrospective
  • sprint-planning - Game sprint planning
  • sprint-status - Game sprint status

Quick Flow:

  • quick-dev - Flexible game development
  • quick-prototype - Rapid prototyping
  • quick-spec - Game spec engineering

Testing (6 workflows):

  • gametest-automate - Automated game test generation
  • gametest-performance - Performance testing
  • gametest-playtest-plan - Playtest planning
  • gametest-test-design - Test design
  • gametest-framework - Test framework initialization
  • gametest-test-review - Test quality review

Workflow Management:

  • workflow-init - Initialize BMAD game projects
  • workflow-status - Status checking

CIS Workflows: Creative Innovation (12)

Section titled “CIS Workflows: Creative Innovation (12)”

Innovation and creativity workflows:

  • Brainstorming sessions
  • Design thinking facilitation
  • Problem-solving workshops
  • Innovation strategy sessions

Workflow Architecture Patterns

Section titled “Workflow Architecture Patterns”

BMAD employs several architectural patterns:

  1. Step-file architecture - Each step loads fresh to combat “lost in the middle” syndrome
  2. Micro-file architecture - Sequential conversation orchestration
  3. Tri-modal workflows - Create, Validate, Edit capabilities
  4. Quad-modal workflows - Brief, Create, Edit, Validate

3. Tasks: 4 Core Tasks

Section titled “3. Tasks: 4 Core Tasks”

Tasks are implemented as XML files in the core module:

TaskDescription
workflowExecute workflow configurations
review-adversarial-generalCynical content review
shard-docSplit large documents into smaller files
index-docsGenerate/update documentation indexes

4. Files: 655 Total Files

Section titled “4. Files: 655 Total Files”

File Distribution

Section titled “File Distribution”
TypeCountPurpose
Markdown (.md)537Documentation, templates, workflows
YAML (.yaml)80Configuration files, workflow definitions
CSV (.csv)24Manifests and data files
XML (.xml)11Task definitions
JSON (.json)2Configuration data

Key Manifest Files

Section titled “Key Manifest Files”
  • agent-manifest.csv - 27 registered agents with metadata
  • workflow-manifest.csv - 68 registered workflows
  • task-manifest.csv - 4 core tasks
  • files-manifest.csv - Complete file inventory (655 files)
  • tool-manifest.csv - Available tools and capabilities

Notable Architectural Patterns

Section titled “Notable Architectural Patterns”

1. Modular Design

Section titled “1. Modular Design”

Clear separation of concerns across domains:

  • Each module operates independently
  • Shared core functionality
  • Extensible architecture

2. Manifest-Driven System

Section titled “2. Manifest-Driven System”

CSV manifests provide:

  • Complete inventory of components
  • Metadata for each agent/workflow/task
  • Version tracking and management

3. Workflow-Centric Execution

Section titled “3. Workflow-Centric Execution”

Agents execute through structured workflows:

  • Consistent patterns across modules
  • State management via variables
  • Sequential progression through phases

4. Quality Assurance Built-In

Section titled “4. Quality Assurance Built-In”
  • Adversarial code reviews in all modules
  • Implementation readiness validation
  • Test architecture specialists
  • Sprint retrospectives and corrections

Recommended Use Cases

Section titled “Recommended Use Cases”

BMM Best For

Section titled “BMM Best For”
  • Traditional software development projects
  • Business analysis and requirement gathering
  • Enterprise application development
  • Teams following agile methodologies

BMGD Best For

Section titled “BMGD Best For”
  • Game development (Unity, Unreal, Godot)
  • Indie game developers
  • Game design documentation
  • Game testing and quality assurance

BMB Best For

Section titled “BMB Best For”
  • Creating custom BMAD agents
  • Building new BMAD modules
  • Workflow architecture design
  • System integration

CIS Best For

Section titled “CIS Best For”
  • Innovation workshops
  • Design thinking sessions
  • Problem-solving breakthroughs
  • Creative brainstorming

Unique Features

Section titled “Unique Features”

Party Mode

Section titled “Party Mode”

Multi-agent conversations for complex decision-making through agent collaboration.

Quick Flow

Section titled “Quick Flow”

Rapid development methodologies for fast iteration and prototyping.

Game-Specific Tools

Section titled “Game-Specific Tools”

Specialized workflows for game development needs, from design to testing.

Creative Suite

Section titled “Creative Suite”

Innovation and design thinking tools for creative problem-solving.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

BMAD represents a sophisticated approach to multi-agent systems, with:

  • 26 agents across 4 specialized domains
  • 68 workflows covering complete development lifecycles
  • 4 core tasks for system operations
  • 655 files implementing the entire platform

The modular design, manifest-driven architecture, and workflow-centric execution make BMAD a powerful tool for teams looking to leverage AI agents across diverse domains—from traditional software development to game creation and creative innovation.

Whether you’re building enterprise applications, developing games, or facilitating innovation workshops, BMAD has specialized agents and workflows designed to help you succeed.

References

Section titled “References”
  • BMAD Documentation
  • BMAD Getting Started Guide
  • BMAD Upgrade Experience
Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Agents
  • Workflow Automation
  • Game Development
  • Software Architecture

SkillMaster: The Premier Agent Skill Marketplace for Claude, Codex & ChatGPT

Jan 15, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

The AI agent ecosystem is undergoing a fundamental transformation in 2026. As AI coding assistants like Claude Code, OpenAI’s Codex CLI, and ChatGPT become increasingly sophisticated, the ability to extend their capabilities through reusable skills has emerged as a critical paradigm shift. Enter SkillMaster—the premier marketplace for verified AI agent skills.

What is SkillMaster?

Section titled “What is SkillMaster?”

SkillMaster.cc is a curated marketplace that hosts production-ready skill packages compatible with multiple AI coding platforms. Unlike GitHub repositories that require manual integration, SkillMaster provides:

  • Verified Skills: Each skill package is audited for security and quality
  • Cross-Platform Compatibility: Skills work across Claude Code, Codex CLI, and ChatGPT
  • One-Click Installation: Streamlined setup process without manual configuration
  • Version Control: Tracked updates and compatibility notes

The Agent Skills Revolution

Section titled “The Agent Skills Revolution”

The concept of “Agent Skills” represents a significant evolution in how we interact with AI coding assistants. Rather than crafting custom prompts for each task, developers can now:

  1. Discover pre-built skills for specific domains
  2. Install them directly into their AI assistant
  3. Invoke skills with simple commands

This approach mirrors the package management revolution that transformed programming languages—from manual dependency management to npm, pip, and cargo.

Key Skill Categories on SkillMaster

Section titled “Key Skill Categories on SkillMaster”

Based on the available skill collection, SkillMaster organizes skills into several categories:

Development & Design

Section titled “Development & Design”
  • algorithmic-art: Create generative art with p5.js using seeded randomness
  • canvas-design: Produce visual art in PNG/PDF formats with design philosophy
  • frontend-design: Build production-grade web interfaces with high design quality

Documentation & Content

Section titled “Documentation & Content”
  • doc-coauthoring: Structured workflow for co-authoring documentation
  • docx: Comprehensive Microsoft Word document manipulation
  • pptx: PowerPoint presentation creation and editing
  • pdf: PDF manipulation including text extraction, merging, and form filling

Developer Tools

Section titled “Developer Tools”
  • mcp-builder: Create Model Context Protocol servers for external integrations
  • xlsx: Excel/spreadsheet operations with formulas and data analysis
  • webapp-testing: Playwright toolkit for web application testing
  • code-review: Security, performance, and maintainability analysis

Communication

Section titled “Communication”
  • internal-comms: Internal communication templates (status reports, newsletters)
  • slack-gif-creator: Animated GIF creation optimized for Slack

Theming & Styling

Section titled “Theming & Styling”
  • brand-guidelines: Official brand color and typography application
  • theme-factory: Apply pre-set themes or generate custom themes for artifacts

The SKILL.md Standard

Section titled “The SKILL.md Standard”

SkillMaster, along with other marketplaces like SkillsMP, uses the open SKILL.md format for defining agent skills. This standardization means:

  • Portability: Skills work across different AI platforms
  • Community Contribution: Anyone can create and publish skills
  • Quality Assurance: Standardized format enables automated validation

A typical SKILL.md file includes:

Comparing SkillMarket Ecosystem

Section titled “Comparing SkillMarket Ecosystem”

SkillMaster operates alongside other emerging marketplaces:

MarketplaceFocusPlatform Support
SkillMasterVerified, audited skillsClaude, Codex, ChatGPT
SkillsMP63,000+ skills, open standardClaude Code, Codex CLI, ChatGPT
Skills4AgentsMonetization-focusedClaude Code
Agent Skill NinjaVS Code extension integrationGitHub Copilot, Claude Code

How to Get Started

Section titled “How to Get Started”

1. Browse the Marketplace

Section titled “1. Browse the Marketplace”

Visit skillmaster.cc to explore available skills. Each skill includes:

  • Description and use cases
  • Compatibility information
  • Installation instructions
  • Version history

2. Install a Skill

Section titled “2. Install a Skill”

Installation typically involves:

3. Use the Skill

Section titled “3. Use the Skill”

Once installed, invoke the skill through natural language:

Creating Your Own Skills

Section titled “Creating Your Own Skills”

The beauty of the Agent Skills ecosystem is its extensibility. To create a skill:

  1. Create a new directory with your skill name
  2. Add a SKILL.md file with instructions
  3. Include any templates or examples
  4. Submit to SkillMaster for verification

For detailed guides, see:

  • Claude Code Skills Documentation
  • How to Build Claude Skills
  • Claude Skills and CLAUDE.md Guide

The Future of Agent Skills

Section titled “The Future of Agent Skills”

As we move through 2026, several trends are emerging:

  1. Enterprise Adoption: Companies building internal skill libraries
  2. Monetization: Developers earning revenue from premium skills
  3. Cross-Platform Standardization: OpenAI adopting Anthropic’s skills format
  4. AI-Native Development: Skills becoming the primary abstraction for AI-augmented workflows

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

SkillMaster represents more than just a marketplace—it’s a signal of how software development is evolving. By providing a trusted source for verified, production-ready AI agent skills, it lowers the barrier to entry for developers looking to enhance their AI workflows.

Whether you’re looking to accelerate documentation tasks, streamline code reviews, or add creative capabilities to your AI assistant, SkillMaster offers a growing ecosystem of solutions.

References

Section titled “References”
  • SkillMaster.cc - Official website
  • Claude Code Skills Documentation
  • SkillsMP Marketplace - Alternative marketplace with 63K+ skills
  • Anthropic Skills GitHub Repository
  • Build Your First Claude Code Agent Skill - Medium

  • AI Agents
  • Claude Code
  • Skills Marketplace
  • OpenAI Codex
  • ChatGPT
  • Developer Tools

BMAD Method Getting Started: A Practical Guide to Quick-Dev Workflows

Jan 14, 2026
Vibe Sparking

BMAD Method (Breakthrough Method of Agile AI‑Driven Development) is an AI-driven development framework that turns “random prompt hacking” into a repeatable delivery system.

It does that by packaging:

  • Specialized agents (PM/Architect/Dev/QA/Docs…)
  • Guided workflows (discovery → planning → execution → verification)
  • Living artifacts (briefs, PRDs, stories, checklists)

…so your project context and quality gates live in the repo, not in someone’s chat history.

1) Features & highlights (what you get)

Section titled “1) Features & highlights (what you get)”

Role-based help instead of ad-hoc prompting

Section titled “Role-based help instead of ad-hoc prompting”

BMAD provides role personas so you can reliably ask for:

  • a product-level framing (PM)
  • architecture tradeoffs and decisions (Architect)
  • implementation guidance and code changes (Dev)
  • verification and test strategy (QA/Test Architect)
  • documentation and release notes (Tech Writer)

Scale-adaptive planning

Section titled “Scale-adaptive planning”

BMAD is designed to “right-size” the process:

  • Quick Dev / Quick Flow: bug fixes, small features, quick iterations
  • Standard track: medium features/products, PRD → architecture → stories
  • Enterprise track: governance-heavy environments, stronger gates and traceability

A closed loop from idea to verification

Section titled “A closed loop from idea to verification”

Instead of generating one-off answers, BMAD encourages a workflow that produces durable artifacts and then uses them to implement and verify.

2) Prerequisites, installation & configuration

Section titled “2) Prerequisites, installation & configuration”

What you’ll need

Section titled “What you’ll need”

BMAD works with any AI coding assistant that supports custom system prompts / project context. Common choices include:

  • Claude Code (CLI)
  • Cursor
  • Windsurf
  • Roo Code (VS Code)
  • GitHub Copilot (VS Code)

Installation (typical)

Section titled “Installation (typical)”

From your project root (Node.js 20+ recommended), install BMAD into the project (not your home dir) so the config is versionable:

After installation, your repo will contain BMAD modules (agents/workflows/templates). From there you can run workflows from your IDE/assistant.

3) Quick Dev workflow (the “fast lane”)

Section titled “3) Quick Dev workflow (the “fast lane”)”

If your BMAD setup exposes slash commands, you can start here:

If you just want to ship something quickly, the mindset is:

  1. Align on the goal and constraints
  2. Produce a minimal plan / acceptance criteria
  3. Implement with tight verification loops

A practical “Quick Dev” run looks like:

Step A — Define the outcome

Section titled “Step A — Define the outcome”

Write down:

  • What “done” means (acceptance criteria)
  • Guardrails (what not to change)
  • How you’ll verify (build/tests/manual steps)

Step B — Use the workflow to generate lightweight artifacts

Section titled “Step B — Use the workflow to generate lightweight artifacts”

Use the Quick Dev workflow to produce a small set of artifacts that are actually useful during implementation:

  • a short task breakdown
  • an implementation checklist
  • a verification checklist

Step C — Execute and verify

Section titled “Step C — Execute and verify”

Have the Dev implement changes and the QA/Test Architect verify with:

  • targeted tests
  • build checks
  • smoke tests

The biggest win is that these outputs are reusable: they become a baseline for the next iteration.

4) Recommended scenarios & daily workflow examples

Section titled “4) Recommended scenarios & daily workflow examples”

Scenario 1 — Fix a bug without losing the plot

Section titled “Scenario 1 — Fix a bug without losing the plot”

Use Quick Dev to:

  • reproduce the bug
  • add a minimal regression test
  • fix the root cause
  • verify the build

Scenario 2 — Add a small feature with predictable scope

Section titled “Scenario 2 — Add a small feature with predictable scope”

Use Quick Dev to:

  • clarify edge cases
  • define acceptance criteria
  • implement incrementally
  • validate UX/behavior quickly

Scenario 3 — Personal knowledge work (docs, blog posts, learning)

Section titled “Scenario 3 — Personal knowledge work (docs, blog posts, learning)”

BMAD isn’t only for code. A simple pattern:

  • Tech Writer drafts an outline + key sections
  • Dev/Architect validate technical correctness
  • QA proofreads for completeness and broken steps

This is especially effective for “tutorial + troubleshooting + references” style posts.

5) Precautions, FAQ & troubleshooting

Section titled “5) Precautions, FAQ & troubleshooting”

“The agent output is too verbose / too shallow”

Section titled ““The agent output is too verbose / too shallow””
  • Ensure your experience level is configured appropriately (expert vs beginner).
  • Use acceptance criteria to constrain the output.
  • Prefer artifacts (brief/spec/story) over free-form chat.

“Context is too large / token limits”

Section titled ““Context is too large / token limits””

BMAD’s document sharding and story-driven approach helps. Keep artifacts scoped and feed only what’s needed to the next role.

“My team doesn’t want heavy ceremony”

Section titled ““My team doesn’t want heavy ceremony””

Start with Quick Dev. If it sticks, gradually adopt more planning where it pays off.

6) Collaboration & comparisons

Section titled “6) Collaboration & comparisons”

BMAD is in the same family of ideas as other “spec-first / structured prompting” approaches (e.g., OpenSpec, Spec Kit), but BMAD’s distinguishing traits are:

  • explicit role personas
  • explicit workflow definitions
  • strong focus on artifact flow and repeatability

7) References

Section titled “7) References”
  • Official docs: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • BMAD Method site: https://bmad-method.org
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Workflow
  • AI Agents
  • Agile
  • Vibe Coding

ClawdHub - A Fast Skill Registry for AI Agents with Vector Search

Jan 6, 2026
Vibe Sparking

ClawdHub - A Fast Skill Registry for AI Agents with Vector Search

Section titled “ClawdHub - A Fast Skill Registry for AI Agents with Vector Search”

In the rapidly evolving ecosystem of AI agents and coding assistants, finding the right skills and tools can be challenging. Enter ClawdHub—a fast, modern skill registry for AI agents that leverages vector search to help you discover exactly what you need.

Introduction and Key Features

Section titled “Introduction and Key Features”

ClawdHub is a specialized registry designed for the AI agent ecosystem. What sets it apart is its intelligent use of vector search technology, enabling semantic discovery of skills rather than relying solely on keyword matching.

What Makes ClawdHub Special?

Section titled “What Makes ClawdHub Special?”
  • Vector Search Capability: Find skills by meaning, not just exact keywords
  • Curated Signal: Highlighted “Featured” skills for quick trust and quality assurance
  • Real-time Updates: Latest drops section shows newly uploaded skills
  • Agent-Focused: Specifically designed for AI agent workflows like Claude Code, Codex, and other assistants
  • Developer-Friendly: Clean interface for browsing and discovering productivity-enhancing tools

Featured Skills on ClawdHub

Section titled “Featured Skills on ClawdHub”

Highlighted (Curated for Quality)

Section titled “Highlighted (Curated for Quality)”

CalDAV Calendar

Section titled “CalDAV Calendar”

Sync and query CalDAV calendars (iCloud, Google, Fastmail, Nextcloud, etc.) using vdirsyncer + khal. Works seamlessly on Linux systems.

Trello

Section titled “Trello”

Manage Trello boards, lists, and cards directly through the Trello REST API—perfect for project management automation.

Slack

Section titled “Slack”

Control Slack from Clawdbot with capabilities including reacting to messages, pinning/unpinning items in channels and DMs.

Latest Skill Additions

Section titled “Latest Skill Additions”

Clinkding - Linkding Bookmark Integration

Section titled “Clinkding - Linkding Bookmark Integration”

Manage Linkding bookmarks efficiently—save URLs, search, tag, organize, and retrieve your personal bookmark collection. Ideal for users who want to save links, search bookmarks, manage tags, or organize their reading list.

Granola Meeting Transcripts

Section titled “Granola Meeting Transcripts”

Access Granola meeting transcripts and notes directly through your AI agent workflow.

Model Usage

Section titled “Model Usage”

Leverage CodexBar CLI for local cost usage tracking—summarize per-model usage for Codex or Claude, including current model breakdowns or full model statistics.

1Password

Section titled “1Password”

Set up and use 1Password CLI (op) for seamless secret management—install the CLI, enable desktop app integration, sign in (single or multi-account), and read/inject/run secrets via op.

JIRA

Section titled “JIRA”

Manage Jira issues, boards, sprints, and projects via jira-cli. Search, create, update, and transition issues directly from the command line.

Pi Orchestration

Section titled “Pi Orchestration”

Orchestrate multiple AI models (GLM, MiniMax, etc.) as workers using Pi Coding Agent with Claude as coordinator—powerful for multi-model workflows.

TMDb - Movie Database

Section titled “TMDb - Movie Database”

Search movies and TV shows, get cast information, ratings, streaming info, and personalized recommendations via TMDb API.

Crypto Tracker

Section titled “Crypto Tracker”

Track crypto prices, set alerts, and search coins via CoinGecko API—perfect for cryptocurrency enthusiasts.

Voice Transcribe

Section titled “Voice Transcribe”

Transcribe audio files using OpenAI’s gpt-4o-mini-transcribe model with vocabulary hints and text replacements. Requires uv for package management.

Video Transcript Downloader

Section titled “Video Transcript Downloader”

Download videos, audio, subtitles, and clean paragraph-style transcripts from YouTube and any yt-dlp supported site. Essential for content creators and researchers.

SwiftUI View Refactor

Section titled “SwiftUI View Refactor”

Refactor and review SwiftUI view files for consistent structure, dependency injection, and Observation usage—clean up layout, handle view models safely, and standardize dependencies.

SwiftUI Performance Audit

Section titled “SwiftUI Performance Audit”

Audit and improve SwiftUI runtime performance through code review and architecture—diagnose slow rendering, janky scrolling, high CPU/memory usage, and excessive view updates.

How Vector Search Enhances Skill Discovery

Section titled “How Vector Search Enhances Skill Discovery”

Traditional skill registries rely on keyword matching, which can be limiting. If you search for “calendar management” but a skill is tagged with “schedule events,” you might miss it entirely.

ClawdHub’s vector search solves this by:

  • Understanding semantic meaning—search queries match related concepts
  • Contextual relevance—finds skills based on what you’re trying to accomplish
  • Multilingual support—language barriers become less significant

Best Practices for Using ClawdHub

Section titled “Best Practices for Using ClawdHub”

For Skill Consumers

Section titled “For Skill Consumers”
  1. Use Natural Language Queries: Describe what you want to accomplish rather than exact keywords
  2. Check Highlighted Skills First: Curated skills are typically more reliable and well-maintained
  3. Review Latest Drops: Stay updated with new additions that might solve emerging problems

For Skill Publishers

Section titled “For Skill Publishers”
  1. Provide Clear Descriptions: Detailed descriptions help vector search understand your skill’s purpose
  2. Use Meaningful Names: Names that reflect functionality improve discoverability
  3. Keep Skills Updated: Fresh, working skills gain traction in the community

Integration with AI Workflows

Section titled “Integration with AI Workflows”

ClawdHub skills are designed to work seamlessly with popular AI coding assistants:

  • Claude Code: Install skills to extend Claude’s capabilities
  • Codex CLI: Enhance OpenAI’s Codex with specialized tools
  • Other Agent Frameworks: Many skills follow standard patterns for broad compatibility

Comparison with Other Skill Registries

Section titled “Comparison with Other Skill Registries”
FeatureClawdHubSkillsMPclaude-plugins.dev
Vector Search✅ Yes❌ No❌ No
Curated Highlighting✅ Yes⚠️ Limited⚠️ Basic
Latest Drops✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes
Agent-Specific Focus✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes

Future of ClawdHub

Section titled “Future of ClawdHub”

As the AI agent ecosystem matures, registries like ClawdHub will play an increasingly important role:

  • Standardization: Emerging standards for skill packaging and distribution
  • Community Curation: Enhanced rating and review systems
  • Cross-Platform Compatibility: Skills that work across multiple agent frameworks
  • Advanced Search: Better filtering, categorization, and recommendation algorithms

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

ClawdHub represents a thoughtful approach to skill discovery in the AI agent ecosystem. By combining vector search with human curation, it strikes a balance between automation and quality control—making it easier for developers and power users to find exactly the tools they need.

Whether you’re looking to automate your calendar, manage JIRA tickets, transcribe audio, or refactor SwiftUI code, ClawdHub’s growing collection of skills has something to offer.

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • ClawdHub Official Site
  • ClawdHub CalDAV Calendar Skill
  • ClawdHub Trello Skill
  • ClawdHub Slack Skill

Published: January 6, 2026

  • tools
  • clawdhub
  • ai-agents

Context Graphs: What the Ontology Debate Gets Wrong

Jan 6, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Context Graphs: What the Ontology Debate Gets Wrong

Section titled “Context Graphs: What the Ontology Debate Gets Wrong”

Source: @KirkMarple | Published: January 6, 2026

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

The context graph conversation has exploded over the past two weeks. Foundation Capital’s “Context Graphs: AI’s Trillion-Dollar Opportunity” kicked it off. Animesh Koratana’s thread on coordinate systems deepened the technical framing. Daniel Davis published “The Context Graph Manifesto” connecting it to the semantic web’s heritage.

The conversation is valuable. The questions being asked—how do we give agents organizational memory? how do we capture decision traces? how do we build systems that learn from how work actually happens?—are the right questions.

But Kirk Marple, who has been building context infrastructure for over three years, argues there’s a false dichotomy emerging that misses the point entirely.

The False Dichotomy

Section titled “The False Dichotomy”

The current conversation has crystallized around a binary choice: prescribed ontologies vs learned ontologies.

On one side: Palantir. The argument goes that Palantir built a $400B+ company on prescribed ontologies—define the schema upfront, map messy enterprise data into it, deploy forward engineers to customize it for each customer. It works, but it’s expensive, slow, and doesn’t scale.

On the other side: The future. Learned ontologies. Structure that emerges from how work actually happens rather than how someone designed it to happen. Agent trajectories as training data. Decision traces that compile into organizational world models. The ontology isn’t declared—it’s discovered.

As Jaya Gupta recently wrote:

“The next $50B company will be built on learned ontologies. Structure that emerges from how work actually happens, not how you designed it to happen… Memory assumes you know what to store and how to retrieve it. But the most valuable context is structure you didn’t know existed until agents discovered it through use.”

Here’s the problem: This framing ignores twenty years of ontology work that already exists.

The Third Option: Adopt, Then Learn

Section titled “The Third Option: Adopt, Then Learn”

What’s missing from the prescribed vs learned framing is a third option that’s been hiding in plain sight:

Adopt what exists. Extend where needed. Focus learning on what’s genuinely novel.

The conversation proceeds as if ontology is either an expensive custom artifact (Palantir) or an emergent property of sufficient agent runs (the learned vision). But there’s twenty years of production ontology work that neither camp is acknowledging.

The Ontologies That Already Exist

Section titled “The Ontologies That Already Exist”

Before we talk about learning ontology, it’s worth acknowledging how much of this work already exists in production systems.

Schema.org

Section titled “Schema.org”

Schema.org is a collaborative vocabulary for structured data on the web, founded in 2011 by Google, Microsoft, Yahoo, and Yandex. It defines canonical types for the entities that matter:

  • Person
  • Organization
  • Place
  • Event
  • Product
  • CreativeWork
  • And hundreds more

This isn’t theoretical. It’s in production across billions of web pages. When you see rich snippets in Google search results—event dates, product prices, recipe ratings—that’s Schema.org markup being parsed and displayed.

The types are well-defined. A Person has a name, a jobTitle, a worksFor relationship to an Organization. An Organization has a name, a location, employees. An Event has a startDate, endDate, location, organizer.

This isn’t a custom ontology someone has to build. It exists. It’s maintained. It’s designed for exactly the kind of entity modeling that context graphs require.

WAND and Microsoft Common Data Model

Section titled “WAND and Microsoft Common Data Model”

Here’s something surprising: Microsoft’s Common Data Model—the schema that powers Dynamics 365, Power Platform, and much of Microsoft’s enterprise stack—was licensed from WAND.

WAND has been building enterprise taxonomies and ontologies for decades. Their work predates the current AI wave by years. When Microsoft needed a canonical data model for enterprise entities—Account, Contact, Lead, Opportunity, Case, Product, Campaign—they didn’t build it from scratch. They licensed it from people who had already done the work.

CDM defines the entities that enterprise software operates on. Not in theory—in production, across thousands of organizations running Microsoft’s business applications.

Industry-Specific Standards

Section titled “Industry-Specific Standards”

Beyond Schema.org and CDM, there are industry-specific ontologies refined over years of real-world use:

  • Healthcare: FHIR for clinical data exchange, SNOMED for medical terminology
  • Finance: FIBO for financial instruments and business entities
  • Manufacturing, logistics, energy: Each has domain-specific standards

These aren’t academic exercises. They’re production infrastructure. FHIR, for instance, defines how to represent patients, conditions, medications, observations, and clinical workflows.

What Palantir Actually Does

Section titled “What Palantir Actually Does”

Daniel Davis made an important observation:

“I don’t know why everyone thinks Palantir has ontology tech. They do not. They take months, if not years, building it with ‘forward deployed engineers’. This is not the way to achieve context graphs at scale.”

This is worth unpacking. Palantir’s expense isn’t because “prescribed ontologies are hard.” It’s because they build custom for every deployment. They’re not leveraging existing standards—they’re creating bespoke models for each customer’s specific data landscape.

That’s a business model choice, not an inherent property of prescribed ontologies. You can adopt Schema.org types, extend with CDM patterns, and add domain-specific entities without spending years on custom modeling.

The Palantir approach is expensive because it’s bespoke consulting dressed up as platform. The prescribed ontology itself isn’t the bottleneck. The bottleneck is refusing to leverage what already exists.

Where Learned Ontologies Actually Help

Section titled “Where Learned Ontologies Actually Help”

The learned ontology vision conflates two very different problems:

  1. Entity modeling: What types of things exist and how do they relate? (Solved—or at least solvable with existing standards)
  2. Organizational intelligence: How does this specific organization actually work? What patterns govern decisions? (Genuinely novel, genuinely requires learning)

The entities themselves—Person, Organization, Account, Contact, Event—don’t need to be learned. We know what these are. The types are stable. The core relationships are well-understood.

Where inference genuinely helps is discovering which custom fields actually matter, which relationships get used, which objects are central vs peripheral to how this specific team works. That’s organizational intelligence on top of a foundation—not the foundation itself.

Specifically, the organizational-specific layer:

  • Which contacts at an account actually matter for this deal?
  • What’s the real decision-making process (vs. the org chart)?
  • Which exceptions get approved and which don’t?
  • What precedents actually govern how decisions are made?

This is tacit knowledge. It’s not in any ontology because it’s specific to how this organization operates. It lives in people’s heads, in Slack threads, in the pattern of which escalations get resolved how.

This is where learning from trajectories makes sense. Not “discover that Account is an entity” but “discover that Sarah Chen is the actual decision-maker even though the CRM says it’s her boss.”

The Bootstrap Problem

Section titled “The Bootstrap Problem”

Here’s where the learned ontology vision runs into practical trouble: you can’t learn from agent trajectories until agents are running effectively. But agents can’t run effectively without foundational context.

“You can’t wait for thousands of agentic RAG runs to ‘discover’ that Sarah Chen is a person who works at Acme Corp. You need to know that before agents start reasoning. Otherwise every agent trajectory is fighting the identity resolution problem anew—and you’re paying for that confusion in tokens, latency, and errors.”

The node2vec analogy actually illustrates this. Node2vec learns embeddings from walk patterns over existing edges. It doesn’t discover nodes and edges from scratch. The graph must exist first.

Agentic workflows operate the same way. The agent harness orchestrates tool calls over a pre-built map. The map is operational context: resolved entities, established relationships, temporal state. Build the map first, then agents walk it effectively.

So how do you bootstrap? The learned ontology vision doesn’t have a clean answer. “Run agents and let structure emerge” assumes you have something to run agents over.

The practical answer: adopt existing ontologies as the foundation, then let learning refine and extend.

What’s Actually Unsolved

Section titled “What’s Actually Unsolved”

If entity modeling is largely solved by existing standards, what’s actually novel? What’s the hard problem that context graphs need to address?

It’s not the nouns. It’s the temporal and decision layer.

Temporal Validity

Section titled “Temporal Validity”

Most systems can tell you what’s true now. Almost none can tell you what was true at a specific point in the past, or how truth evolved over time.

“Paula works at Microsoft” is a fact. But it’s not a complete fact. When did she start? Does she still work there? What was true in 2022?

This is what Animesh Koratana called the “event clock”—the infrastructure for tracking not just current state, but how state changes over time. Facts with validAt and invalidAt timestamps. The ability to query “What did we believe about this account when we made that decision?”

Existing ontologies don’t solve this. Schema.org defines Person and Organization, but it doesn’t have native primitives for “this relationship was true from date X to date Y.”

Decision Traces

Section titled “Decision Traces”

Foundation Capital’s original piece got this right: the reasoning that connects data to action has never been captured as data.

When a renewal agent proposes a 20% discount despite a 10% policy cap, it pulls context from multiple systems. Finance approves. The CRM records “20% discount.” Everything that made that decision legible—the inputs, the policy evaluation, the exception route, the approval chain—disappears.

Capturing decision traces isn’t an ontology problem in the traditional sense. It’s an instrumentation problem. You need to be in the execution path where decisions are committed, capturing not just what happened but why it was allowed to happen.

Fact Resolution

Section titled “Fact Resolution”

When you extract facts from content, you get assertions—not necessarily truth. Content can be wrong, outdated, or contradictory.

“The customer is renewing” might appear in a March email. “The customer churned” might appear in a September Slack thread. Both are facts extracted from content. But they can’t both be currently true.

Fact resolution—determining what’s canonical, what’s superseded, what’s corroborated—is genuinely hard. It requires judgment about temporal validity, source authority, and how later information updates earlier assertions.

Why This Matters for Builders

Section titled “Why This Matters for Builders”

The “learned ontology” framing suggests you should focus on trajectory capture, embedding learning, emergent structure. Build the infrastructure for agents to discover ontology through use.

Kirk Marple suggests something different: focus on what’s actually unsolved.

  • Entity modeling? Adopt existing standards. Don’t spend months defining what an Account is. Microsoft already licensed that from WAND.
  • Temporal validity? This is where the work is. Facts that change over time. The ability to query historical state. The event clock.
  • Decision traces? This is where the opportunity is. Being in the execution path. Capturing reasoning as data.
  • Fact resolution? This is where the hard AI problems live. Using LLMs to determine what’s canonical, what’s superseded.

The Semantic Web Finally Won

Section titled “The Semantic Web Finally Won”

There’s a through-line in this conversation that deserves acknowledgment. The semantic web community—RDF, OWL, linked data—has been working on these problems for decades. Schema.org emerged from that tradition.

The vision of machines reasoning over structured, interoperable data wasn’t invented by the AI crowd in 2024.

What killed adoption wasn’t the ideas. It was the syntax. RDF, SPARQL, OWL—the learning curve was brutal, and most developers never climbed it.

LLMs changed that. They can read JSON-LD natively. They understand Schema.org types from training data. They reason over structured data without needing custom query languages or formal inference engines.

The semantic web’s concepts won. The syntax lost. And that’s fine—JSON-LD gives you the benefits without the tax.

Three Principles for Context Graph Builders

Section titled “Three Principles for Context Graph Builders”

  1. Adopt foundations, don’t reinvent them. The entity modeling problem is solved well enough. Don’t spend innovation budget rediscovering it.

  2. Build the temporal layer. Facts with validity periods. Queryable history. Time as a first-class dimension.

  3. Focus learning on what’s genuinely novel. Organizational patterns. Decision dynamics. The tacit knowledge specific to how this organization works. That’s where trajectories help—on top of a foundation, not instead of one.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

The context graph vision is achievable. The question is whether we build on what exists or waste years rediscovering it.

The prescribed vs learned dichotomy is too clean. The entities don’t need to be learned—they need to be adopted: Schema.org, CDM, WAND. The work has been done.

What does need to be built is the temporal and decision layer. Facts that change over time. Reasoning captured as data. Resolution that maintains coherence. That’s genuinely novel. That’s where the opportunity lives.

Resources

Section titled “Resources”

Referenced Articles

Section titled “Referenced Articles”
  • Foundation Capital: Context Graphs: AI’s Trillion-Dollar Opportunity
  • Animesh Koratana: Coordinate Systems Thread
  • Daniel Davis: The Context Graph Manifesto
  • Graphlit: The Context Layer AI Agents Actually Need
  • Graphlit: Building the Event Clock

Standards & Ontologies

Section titled “Standards & Ontologies”
  • Schema.org - Collaborative vocabulary for structured data
  • Microsoft Common Data Model - Enterprise entity definitions
  • WAND Inc - Enterprise taxonomies and ontologies
  • FHIR - Healthcare data exchange standard
  • FIBO - Financial industry business ontology

Related Reading

Section titled “Related Reading”
  • arXiv: Context Graphs as Extension of Knowledge Graphs - June 2024 paper formalizing context graphs with temporal, geographic, and provenance dimensions

Tags:

Oh My OpenCode: Claude Code on Steroids - The Ultimate AI Agent Harness

Jan 4, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

In the rapidly evolving landscape of AI-powered development tools, OpenCode has emerged as a formidable open-source alternative to commercial AI coding assistants. But if you’re a hacker who loves customization and maximum control, Oh My OpenCode is the plugin that transforms OpenCode into something extraordinary.

Created by developer code-yeongyu after spending $24,000 worth of tokens on personal development with various AI tools, Oh My OpenCode represents the distillation of countless hours of testing, configuration, and real-world usage. It’s described by its author as “OpenCode on steroids” — and for good reason.

“If Claude Code does in 7 days what a human does in 3 months, Sisyphus does it in 1 hour. It just works until the task is done. It is a disciplined agent.” — B, Quant Researcher

What is Oh My OpenCode?

Section titled “What is Oh My OpenCode?”

Oh My OpenCode is a comprehensive plugin for OpenCode that adds:

  • Async Subagents — Run multiple AI agents in parallel, just like Claude Code
  • Curated Agents — Pre-configured specialist agents with optimal models
  • Crafted Tools — LSP and AST grep tools for powerful code manipulation
  • Curated MCPs — Built-in Model Context Protocol servers for extended capabilities
  • Claude Code Compatibility — Full compatibility layer for Claude Code configs

The plugin is battle-tested in production environments and has already helped developers accomplish remarkable feats:

“Knocked out 8000 eslint warnings with Oh My Opencode, just in a day” — Jacob Ferrari

“I converted a 45k line tauri app into a SaaS web app overnight using Ohmyopencode and ralph loop.” — James Hargis

Meet Sisyphus: The Default Agent

Section titled “Meet Sisyphus: The Default Agent”

At the heart of Oh My OpenCode is Sisyphus, the default orchestrator agent named after the Greek mythological figure condemned to roll a boulder up a hill for eternity.

“In greek mythology, Sisyphus was condemned to roll a boulder up a hill for eternity as punishment for deceiving the gods. LLM Agents haven’t really done anything wrong, yet they too roll their ‘stones’—their thoughts—every single day. My life is no different. Looking back, we are not so different from these agents.”

Sisyphus’s Toolkit

Section titled “Sisyphus’s Toolkit”

Sisyphus comes equipped with a powerful set of tools and teammates:

Teammates (Curated Agents):

  • Oracle (GPT 5.2 Medium) — Design and debugging
  • Frontend UI/UX Engineer (Gemini 3 Pro) — Frontend development
  • Librarian (Claude Sonnet 4.5) — Official docs and codebase exploration
  • Explore (Grok Code) — Fast codebase exploration with contextual grep

Built-in Tools:

  • Full LSP/AstGrep support for decisive refactoring
  • Todo Continuation Enforcer — Forces agents to finish tasks
  • Comment Checker — Prevents excessive AI-generated comments
  • Claude Code Compatibility — Commands, agents, skills, MCPs, and hooks
  • Curated MCPs: Exa (web search), Context7 (official docs), Grep.app (GitHub search)
  • Async agents with background execution
  • Session management and recovery

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”

1. Multi-Agent Orchestration

Section titled “1. Multi-Agent Orchestration”

Oh My OpenCode enables sophisticated workflows where different AI models work in parallel:

2. LSP and AST Tools

Section titled “2. LSP and AST Tools”

Unlike other AI harnesses that limit agent access to code analysis, Oh My OpenCode gives agents full access to:

  • lsp_hover — Type info and documentation
  • lsp_goto_definition — Jump to symbol definitions
  • lsp_find_references — Find all usages across workspace
  • lsp_document_symbols — Get file symbol outline
  • lsp_workspace_symbols — Search symbols by name
  • lsp_diagnostics — Get errors before building
  • lsp_rename — Rename symbols across workspace
  • ast_grep_search — AST-aware code pattern search
  • ast_grep_replace — AST-aware code replacement

3. Ralph Loop

Section titled “3. Ralph Loop”

Inspired by Anthropic’s Ralph Wiggum plugin, the Ralph Loop is a self-referential development loop that runs until task completion:

The loop detects <promise>DONE</promise> to know when complete and auto-continues if the agent stops prematurely. It supports all programming languages and can be configured with custom iteration limits.

4. Claude Code Compatibility

Section titled “4. Claude Code Compatibility”

Oh My OpenCode provides a comprehensive compatibility layer for Claude Code:

  • Hooks Integration — PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop
  • Config Loaders — Commands, Skills, Agents, MCP configs from standard locations
  • Data Storage — Todo management and transcripts in Claude Code format
  • Compatibility Toggles — Fine-grained control over which features to enable

5. Context Management

Section titled “5. Context Management”

Intelligent context management features:

  • Context Window Monitor — Alerts at 70%+ usage
  • Preemptive Compaction — Compacts session at 85% usage (enabled by default)
  • Anthropic Auto Compact — Automatic summarization at token limits
  • Tool Output Truncator — Prevents one search from eating your entire context

Installation

Section titled “Installation”

Quick Install (Interactive)

Section titled “Quick Install (Interactive)”

Follow the prompts to configure your Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini subscriptions.

Agent-Assisted Install

Section titled “Agent-Assisted Install”

Paste this into a fresh OpenCode session:

The agent will guide you through the entire setup process.

Configuration

Section titled “Configuration”

Configuration is stored in JSON/JSONC format:

User config: ~/.config/opencode/oh-my-opencode.json Project config: .opencode/oh-my-opencode.json

Example Configuration

Section titled “Example Configuration”

Usage Modes

Section titled “Usage Modes”

Keywords for Specialized Modes

Section titled “Keywords for Specialized Modes”

Include these keywords in your prompts to activate specialized modes:

  • ultrawork / ulw — Maximum performance with parallel agent orchestration
  • search / find — Maximized search effort with parallel explore and librarian agents
  • analyze / investigate — Deep analysis mode with multi-phase expert consultation

Calling Specialized Agents

Section titled “Calling Specialized Agents”

Comparison with Claude Code

Section titled “Comparison with Claude Code”
FeatureClaude CodeOh My OpenCode
Multi-model supportClaude onlyClaude, GPT, Gemini, Grok
Async agentsYesYes
LSP toolsAnalysis onlyFull refactoring
HooksYesYes (compatible)
Custom agentsSkills systemCurated + custom
ExtensibilityLimitedHighly extensible
Open sourceNoYes

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Start Simple

Section titled “1. Start Simple”

If the configuration seems overwhelming, just remember: include the word ultrawork in your prompt. The agent will figure out the rest.

2. Use Background Agents for Parallel Work

Section titled “2. Use Background Agents for Parallel Work”

Let different models work on different parts simultaneously:

3. Leverage Context Management

Section titled “3. Leverage Context Management”

Oh My OpenCode aggressively uses multiple agents to lighten context load. Your agent becomes the dev team lead, and you’re the AI manager.

4. Customize for Your Workflow

Section titled “4. Customize for Your Workflow”

You can disable features you don’t need:

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Issue: Bunx fails on Ubuntu/Debian

Section titled “Issue: Bunx fails on Ubuntu/Debian”

If you installed Bun via Snap, bunx will fail due to sandboxing. Use npx instead or reinstall Bun via the official installer.

Issue: OpenCode version compatibility

Section titled “Issue: OpenCode version compatibility”

Ensure OpenCode version is 1.0.150 or higher:

Issue: Authentication errors

Section titled “Issue: Authentication errors”

For Google Gemini, consider using the external opencode-antigravity-auth plugin for multi-account load balancing.

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: Do I need all AI subscriptions?

A: No! You can use Oh My OpenCode with just one provider. The plugin supports Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini, but you pick what you have.

Q: Is this suitable for beginners?

A: The plugin is powerful but can be complex. The author recommends including ultrawork in your prompts — the agent will handle optimization automatically.

Q: How does this compare to AmpCode?

A: Oh My OpenCode is heavily influenced by AmpCode and Claude Code, with features ported and often improved. It’s designed for the open-source OpenCode platform.

Q: Can I use this with existing Claude Code configs?

A: Yes! The Claude Code compatibility layer means your existing configurations, hooks, and skills should work out of the box.

Community and Resources

Section titled “Community and Resources”
  • GitHub Repository: code-yeongyu/oh-my-opencode
  • OpenCode Documentation: opencode.ai/docs
  • Video Review: Oh My OpenCode Is Actually Insane
  • Medium Article: A Deep Dive into Oh My OpenCode
  • Reddit Community: r/opencodeCLI

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Oh My OpenCode represents the culmination of extensive real-world testing and refinement. It transforms OpenCode from a capable AI coding assistant into a sophisticated multi-agent orchestration platform.

Whether you’re a seasoned developer looking to maximize productivity or an AI enthusiast exploring the boundaries of what’s possible, Oh My OpenCode offers a powerful, extensible, and thoroughly tested solution.

As the author puts it:

“Stop agonizing over agent harness choices. I’ll do the research, borrow from the best, and ship updates here.”

Ready to supercharge your OpenCode experience? Install Oh My OpenCode and let Sisyphus keep that boulder rolling.

Sources:

  • Oh My OpenCode GitHub Repository
  • OpenCode Official Site
  • YouTube: Oh My OpenCode Is Actually Insane
  • Medium: A Deep Dive into Oh My OpenCode
